lines.x: Silent. Strange. Unfathomable. Outsider.
So they naturally come to say.
Shadowy. Cold-blooded. Leaves for dead. A born monster.
So all will say behind my back.
Yes.
One needs only lose one's home, and one is nothing at all.
Reason says that I, coming here, should have been able to begin a new life.
Except the past cannot be rid. These things follow your shadow and climb your shins, dragging you into the abyss of the swamp.
Not one of us can break free of the past.
If there comes a day, and your home has found you...
2:33 A.M. \ Overcast
Rhodes Island Landship, Hallway
Alright! Once this report's handed in, your field mission will be formally closed out.
Mm.
Wait, is it still... blank? Ahem, hm-hm...
The only thing on this entire sheet is the word 'complete!'
Hmm?
The mission definitely is complete.
I-I mean, that's not wrong...
...but it wouldn't hurt if you wrote a little more, right?!
Hm?
(What do I do... just how difficult is she?)
(I never really talked to her before. I heard we were linking up with her, and the group leader even warned me... but this is still going to be tricky to handle.)
(Deep breath)
Alright, okay. I understand.
If the Doctor has any questions regarding the particulars of your mission, we'll seek confirmation from you again then.
...The Doctor?
Yes, Dr. Kal'tsit presumed you wouldn't want to hand a report in to her... and she also mentioned Amiya's too soft on you, and it can't go on like this... and so on.
So then we filed this mission away on the Doctor's end.
That woman. Hmph.
Do you want to report directly, then? Looks like the Doctor's out right now, so you can relax for a few days until the Doctor's back.
This report of yours, right now, could seriously use some more fleshing out!
Such a pain.
Fine, then. Mission code F1071, confirming submission. Follow-up specifics—
Wait.
Huh?
Give the files back to me. I've decided, it's okay if I add a few more details.
Oh! Of course it's okay! I'll leave it to you, then.
(This might go smoother than I anticipated...)
That's all from me then. Do head back and get some proper... um?
Your expression just completely changed. Has something happened?
In likelihood.
Huh? In here? Aboard the ship? An intruder?! I've heard about your acute senses. Could it be...
No, that's not right. The alarms should have sounded if there was danger aboard, right?
...Singing.
Singing? What singing? Er, it's silent all around. I can't hear any...
How? Why is there singing here?

I suppose there is a sort of droning sound, but that's not a song, is it? There's all kinds of machinery operating aboard Rhodes Island. Is it their vibrations, just maybe? Are you still not fully used to it?
No. The scent.
The scent of the seawater.
...Skadi? You're scaring me a little. What are you tal...
Huh? Hey! Skadi!
Skadi runs too hurriedly, too rapidly. She runs for the stairwell, fearing that those few seconds of apprehension riding the elevator will allow that scent to slip away.

lines.st: A hunter is walking on seacoast way♪

lines.x: She can hear the song. Aboard this ship, a vast majority cannot.
This language, this melody, has roused her memories. She is not one for nostalgia, but she knows it all too well.
But... this shouldn't be. This is a song that should not arise here.

lines.st: With homeland left a mile behind, they must lament and pray♪

lines.x: The singing shivers all throughout the ship's pipelines.
The scent of seawater, breaking unbreakingly, seeps out from the cabin walls.
Skadi remembers this scent just the same. With the damp closed in on her, her skin continuously contracts, becomes taut. Nervous, yet she is stirred too.
Could she have awoken? She cannot help but hasten her step.
Specter!

lines.st: She pulls open the door to the treatment ward.
The bed is empty.
Perhaps she wanted to take a stroll outside after becoming lucid.

lines.x: Wait... what is this?

lines.st: She picks it up, and rubs it in her fingers.

lines.x: ...It looks like hers.

lines.st: Specter's gold chain had been carelessly thrown to the ground. Or perhaps, deliberately.
Did she despise this item? Then she surely isn't pleased with her current identity.
The affairs of the Church—at least the Church of the sea—are all nefarious ones.
But... something's not right.
Outsider.
Without knowing why, Skadi has abruptly recalled that word.
She realizes that the scent is not Specter's at all.
Compared to the Specter of past, this scent now is too strong, and too callous. Skadi doubts it was simply a surprise that went all too hard to her head.

lines.x: You're here. Who are you? Come out.

lines.st: An Abyssal Hunter. But not Specter. Of all things, there were still other Abyssal Hunters who survived!
But why here?
...Where has Specter gone?

lines.x: No matter who, or what, you are. Come out.

lines.st: She needs to give chase. Something's not right. She senses the other one truly is nearby.
Skadi rushes out the door, and a silhouette flashes by the farthest recess of the corridor.
No... it's two. The singing still remains. The other one still incants.
Specter. That Abyssal Hunter has taken Specter.

lines.x: So laggard.
If you were any slower, we would've been on our way.
Hunter! Who are you?
In one leap, Skadi pivots the corner, involuntarily feeling for any weapon on her she can use.
—What are you up to?
In that instant, before all else, she meets the other by the eyes.
Wait, you're...
Someone that she has never expected.
Skadi.
You seem quite well.
Could... you be the Second Company's—Specter's leader...?
You're... Gladiia...?!
It has been some time, Skadi. You are still able to recollect the Ægir's song, which deeply gratifies me.

lines.st: Skadi cannot pick out even a single trace of 'gratitude' in her expression. This woman, from the first she knew her, has never once shown any change on her face.

The tall woman carries Specter. The one Abyssal Hunter that Skadi understands to have survived the past several years, Specter, now lies on Gladiia's shoulder, dreaming another uncharacteristically tranquil dream.
The instant Specter's leader let slip a word, the singing vanished.

Skadi is dazed for a spell. From the port window rushes arid winds that only dry land could blow, brushing past Skadi's cheeks, almost dry themselves. She thinks to herself—this hunter is still alive, Gladiia is alive. Skadi has finally found another of her own.
No. It is Gladiia who found them. Yet...

lines.x: I thought you were dead. You staked your lives to let us pass. I thought you were all dead.
Life is a precious thing. I am very happy to see that you, too, are alive.
Given Specter was still alive, I'd always been wondering whether or not any more of the Second could have made it...
How did you find this place? Specter... she's still better off staying in the ward.
One of the fiercest hunters in all of Skadi's mind chooses, at this juncture, to say nothing.
In Skadi's heart, doubt burgeons.
She's changed a lot. She may not necessarily recognize you now. Her body is still feeble.
Feeble? A hunter could not be feeble.
How long have you been on dry land?
Disease of the land, of a special kind, has hollowed her body out. The people aboard this ship can stabilize her condition.
In all these years, she's never come back to sanity. The current Specter is another person entirely. I don't know what it was that made her like this.
Gladiia does not speak, and the breeze blows through, her hair drifting in its wakes, covering her eye.
Skadi has realized where the problem lies.
Gladiia. Yes, you're Gladiia. There is no mistake. But what do you intend to do?
Where are you planning to take her?
I believed that upon dry land, the hunters were free. Have I misunderstood something?
No, Gladiia... she's still asleep. You never asked her... whether or not she wants to go.
You want to take her away from here.

You seem to show a great deal of interest in these new colleagues of yours. Do they not treat you too in kind? It would be but a passing motion for you to pinch their spines to dust, yet how is it you do not instill dread in them?
For these people of dry land to treat us with enmity is normal, but not for the ones aboard this ship. The ones I have met would not.
I still cannot fathom why you drop your guard.
No... I don't want to get them mixed up in this matter. The problem doesn't lie with me, nor them.
The problem lies in you.
Skadi takes a step toward.
What is your intent in doing this? You had best explain.
Hunter Skadi.
—On dry land, I will not constrain you with my rank of Consul.
Hm...
Yet neither do I have any obligation to explain myself to you.
I simply believe that I should have you know. It was I who took her, a member of my own company. This suffices.
Why?
You need not know the reason yet this evening.
But Specter!
—(Two Iberian words)—

lines.st: Before the name fully reaches Skadi's ears, Gladiia has already slipped from the ship's window into the moonlight outside, Specter's limp body disappearing with her.
Skadi does not reach out. She does not even move.
She knows well that if Gladiia wants to leave, she has no hope of ever catching her; yet, if Gladiia only wanted to escape, then she would not have left any information behind.
She was seeking help, or she was hoodwinking herself. Skadi half-believes it, half doesn't, yet has no other alternative. At the least, she did not order Skadi to do anything, or not to do anything.
'Sal Viento.'
That is what Gladiia said.
Skadi cannot help but go.

lines.x: Sal Viento.
This is far too unmoving a reunion.
What are you doing here?
Hey, isn't that Skadi?
She looks familiar. A little like the picture.
Ah! Haha, that didn't take any effort at all...
It's Skadi for sure. We hid in the right spot.
Hm?
Hey, see, she responded... and look at her face. That really is Skadi!
Still remember the 'Captain,' Skadi? We've got his money, and now we're gonna avenge him with your life!
You crackbrained idiot... you crackbrained idiot! I said this was a rotten idea!
Didn't we say using his money to run some cons would be enough?! Avenge my ass, bounty my ass! What chance in hell?!
Remember the rumors! If that's really her, you think there's a single cent in this for us? It'll cost our lives!
We'd better scram before it's too late! Grab your dumb scrap and run!
Pah.

You go crawling back to your shitheap bed cuddling burdenbeast piss and yelling mamá. You lose your nerve the moment a single long-haired girl says something to you. You want ME to go home empty-handed? No goddamn way!
You're right! Even if she really is Skadi, even if, what the hell can she do? She's all alone right now, isn't she?
We've got one, two... yeah, a bunch of guys!
But all those powerhouse bounty hunters are rotting in the ground, aren't they? She was alone then too!
And think about it, every time you say you have so many guys, does anything good ever come of it?
And who saw that? If you ask me, those guys? Maybe they just got drunk off their asses one night and all went into the same pretty little Kuranta's room.
Then she got her crossbows out her pants, went free-for-all, blew their brains to mush.
That's what rumors are! If all those wandering singers or whoever went around singing and playing without puffing their stories up a little, you think they'd make enough money to eat?
Splitting fifty mountains with a single sword, yeah, right... and I can punch a hole straight through Londinium with one fist!
Listen to me, brothers! If you wanna eat, come with me!
Skadi, Schmadi, we can put her down right here, and nobody can pinch our gold after! Add the bounty the others put on her head, we'll be set for the rest of our lives plus retirement!
I'll give you a choice. Run now, and you can live. I have no time to waste with you people.
Charge! Kill her dead!
(Ægirian) It's the rotted seagrass that clings most to one's leg.
I never thought it would be the annoyance of worthless bounty hunters that remained ever unchanging.
Skadi.
You've fallen, Skadi. The old you could've made chunks out of them with just a touch.
Mister José, I am not that bloody.
Hard to say. You've changed, then. You even gave them a chance to escape.
Possibly.
What wind blew you back here again? Why were they waiting to spring you—something to do with me?
I don't know.
I hear you're not in our line of work anymore. Joined some... pharma company? What are you shirking good, decent work for to tromp around these rundown outskirts again?
You're well-informed, Mister José. This time I need you to help me out.

lines.st: The old man licks his rolling paper, and pulls out some second-rate tobacco from his pouch.

lines.x: Several years have come and gone. Here I thought you'd folded, seeing as you went through plenty of Kazimierz bounty hunters. This far out from Kazimierz, and I could still hear word of what you'd been up to.
Always thought you only knew how to make big ruckuses.
But you've been quiet as this now.
You came roadrollering in all the way from Minos country like a tow truck, just to find me? Go on, then, tell me what you want.
I need to get to Sal Viento.
Sal... Viento?
Don't tell me. Even you've never heard of the place?
No, I've heard. Out of all us information peddlers, only I must've heard. The question's you—what makes you want to go there?
There's nothing there anymore. Few decades ago... close to nothing left after.
So close to such a vast patch of ocean, and you think anything'd be left? No better than bare frames left, letting the wind and all through.
Many bounty hunters wanting to get up to those abandoned cities, just like beastlings pouncing on a rotting burdenbeast corpse.
All of them thinking there's still meat they can pick off. They don't know whatever they swallow there might as well be a batch of poison.
Iberia. All of them think this unspeaking Iberia's just like Kazimierz and Gaul, treasure all over the map.
Hah, there's nothing there at all. When a place has nothing at all, it's likelier than all it could have anything.
So do you tell those bounty hunters anything?
If they ask me to sell info, I'll sell it. As far as ones like me go, whether those folks come back or don't, it's all business.
Fact is, they don't come back at all. Not one ever comes back.
Two weeks ago, a team came to me. Lackeys of the 'Daggerhorn.'
He had equipment leagues better than all these runts lying around. Very experienced too. Brought more than ten with him. Still haven't seen them again yet.
Very many wonder about where they went. They could've been caught by the Penal Battalion, or it could've been the Iberian Defensa's doing, but I've heard tell of a different possibility.
Over there be... monsters.
Oh.
Or it was the Inquisition that got 'em. I don't know what's a more wretched end.
Monsters or people, my sword draws little distinction. There are only those who block my way, and those who don't.
Alright, alright. You can't be going to Sal Viento for a holiday, that much's for sure.
I need to find someone.
What poor bastard has your wrath this time?
This time's different. It's my... It's someone I knew from before.
Before you even knew me?
Much before.
Here I thought they'd all left. Or died. At least, that's what you told me back in those days.
That's why I'm searching. There's a few things I need to figure out.
You've been searching for quite some years, yeah?
That whole ruckus from last time couldn't have been for any knights' treasure's sakes.
You told me before. People ought to move forward.
Did you bring a treasure map with you?
What?
There're many, some of them even old hands. They travel the barrens, traveling and traveling, and forget where they wanted to go, or what they were even looking for.
They just keep on traveling. They don't know starvation, they don't know exhaustion.
Until the barrenlands do them in.
They were too weak.

Weak? No matter how much stronger, a hunter could still never conquer the boundless barrenlands. So many hunters die for no sure reason out on the road, and don't even know what they're searching for come their deaths—that's not an ending that suits you.
Why are you telling me all this?
As if I've never told you before? They wouldn't rival even the tiniest Ægir machine.
You're strong. So did you save my son's life?
I'm sorry.
Don't say sorry! I'm not trying to flog you here. I'm trying to get you thinking. It's you who hasn't forgiven yourself, not me.
I only regret that I didn't break his legs, stop him following an old papá in his old tracks. Nothing to do with you.
You say you came from the sea. I've never heard of any other sea than the one in Iberia. You're someone I don't know.
So what about you? Is there no one you don't know?
Because that's the kind that killed my son. Someone you don't even know.
That's the sort of thing I'm afraid of.
Never mind it. At least the people you talk about wouldn't spend day and night plotting to kill me. What are you thinking of doing in Sal Viento?
I have a friend who's been taken there.
Strange, that.
Take this. Map.
Alright. What do I need to give you?
Give me?
You sell intel. One thing for another. You've given me information, I'll give you what you need.
(Flicks cigarette ash)
Let me think... That year you brought my young Juan's body back, you handed him to me. Head and arms all still attached to him. So, now we're even.
Mister José, my greatest thanks.
I'll be going, then. Take care.
Stop! Are you in such a hurry to get yourself killed?!
Hm?
Hey. Tell me—has anyone ever said anything like that to you before?
No.
Then you need somebody to tell you well.
You're strolling straight into an Iberian city dressed in all your bounty hunter clothes?
Yes.
You really haven't changed, Skadi. Here I am parching my mouth, and I might as well be the wind for all the good telling you anything does.
The Iberian Defensa'd have you ten times over for looking like that!
And how much time are you ready to waste on them? How much longer can the person you're looking for wait? Iberians aren't like these lousy bounty hunters. They're not that easy to break clean of.
You want to suggest something to me.
I can lend you a few things. Take this harp.
Harp?
Can you play? I remember you played before.
I can.
Good, all the better. Would be fine if you couldn't, long as you pulled off the act.
Change into these too.
What clothes are these? They're very strange.
Whether you're used to them isn't the point. Iberians are.
From now on, you're the wandering singer Skadi.
Go where you want now, singer. Follow the map, go by the waterways, and you'll get there soon enough. This you can do, Ægir.
Farewell, Mister José.
Skadi!
Don't you die out there. You have to die with someone, someone you know.
Right.
Help—Help me!
What are these things... they—they're tangling me, they won't let go! Chopping them apart doesn't even work. Is the entire coast all monsters like this?
Hey—get away, get the hell away!
I wouldn't have come if I goddamn knew! Ja pierdolę, 'Yellow Smog,' bastard, if only you weren't so useless to get hoodwinked back then, would I still be stuck picking up garbage out here?!
Skurwysyny, if I'm not dead by the end of this I'll wish I was! Goddamned Iberia! Country of the actual goddamned!
What—What the hell was that? Turning them all to paste in one shot?
Huff... huff. Whatever it was, at least it lets me live another day—
Ugh—
You. You're not leaving.
And—And who are you? Where the hell does Iberia get all these freaks from?
Second time. Iberia forbids the spreading of rumors. The next you move to speak, be much more careful.
What? Look at that getup, that weapon—
Holy shit. You, you're... that's it, I'm done for, I'm...
Commander. The coast is clean now.
This place is void of all living—er, excepting this outsider who can't stand still and stinks to high Heaven.
What did you see?
Please, I haven't done anything! I swear it, I, I was only lost, I was too careless to end up here, I didn't mean to...
Look, I have gold coins, and plenty of other stuff too, damnit, I've lost so much... Take this money, this equipment, all of it, I just beg you to let me go!
Gather your scraps. When the Commander asks you a question, it's not to hear you blather, outsider.
Question? What question? I swear, honor, I wouldn't dare ask questions... I've got no questions, I guarantee...
I beg you, honor, I don't want... Don't take me... I'd be better off if I just died!
Casting yourself into the sea in front of me? Do you still think it's not too late?
That sword... my hand... too close... wargh...
Now, have you regained some sense? You outsiders are as good as insects in the weeds, hearts avaricious and blackened, yet always so quick to fall to pieces.
Ugh, ah... I'm not running anywhere, honor, I'll tell you anything.
Good. I do not like repeating my questions. This will therefore be the final time.
What did you see on the coast?
I, I... *cough* *cough*... I saw monsters.
How many of you came?
Originally twelve... uh, counting the guide, we're thirteen total.
Commander. He's not lying. I've found the corpses myself along the coast.
Hey. What use will crying be for you now? If it weren't for you all intruding, they wouldn't have sought you out. When you violate the law, you should anticipate the costs.
That said, twelve have died... They've been proliferating in number, all along the way here.
They can't have gone too far from their nest.
This serves to show your judgment was sound. The closer one approaches, the more numerous they are. There is surely something amiss going on in that town!
You may depart.
And this fellow? He has seen much he shouldn't have.
Spare... Spare me! I've told you everything I can! Sir, Commander, your honor... ohh, mama...
What... did you see?
We made an agreement.
I do not know what sense lies in such conduct.
You agreed to it.
Agreed? Pledged—(Bones and flesh chafe against each other.)—promised? To prior be granted an act, and afterwards to accomplish one different?
No.
To such requests of yours, I will respond. No agreement, nor promise.
A response... but of course.
Honorable herald—
That you will to respond is already my honor.
3:23 P.M. \ Overcast
Iberia, Sal Viento

lines.st: A city, life long lost, lies half-buried under dust and whiff of sea breeze.

lines.x: This is the place on the map.
I can't say it smells any good.
Hello.
May I ask—
(He hangs his head low, and does not speak.)

lines.st: Nobody responds. Nobody so much as turns a head. Their eyes are open, yet all of them are motionless.
Their only trait unlike a statue is that they breathe. Long and slow, they breathe.

lines.x: Monsters?

lines.st: They are all human in appearance. Aside from being far too slender, and clothed in near-rags, they are indistinguishable from the Iberians met along the way, a far cry from the monsters Old José described.

lines.x: They're all humans.
(She utters Iberian.)
Is this not how they speak anymore?
(Iberian again, the accent somewhat changed.)

lines.st: Still no one replies.
Skadi subconsciously grips her case's handle.
Outside, she has her ways of making those who will not talk, talk. But it requires that the target comprehend her speech.

lines.x: Moreover, I'm a singer right now.
A singer doesn't do that. Mister José said so.
Ninety-nine...

lines.st: A small shadow flits across the street.

lines.x: Someone's talking.

lines.st: With a fast step, she chases after.

lines.x: Ninety-nine.
A... child? He's mumbling a number.
I can't have misheard.
You—
My necklace just...
You will not escape!

lines.st: The child slips free of her grasp. With a flick of his head, he scampers down an even deeper alleyway.

lines.x: Ninety-nine, ninety-nine...
You can't run.
*Whimper*...
Why take what's mine? Why bring me here?
Hm?

lines.st: Skadi tramples directly onto a trap laid on the ground.

lines.x: A hole, a few ropes. The ambushes outside were at least much more adequate than this.
Come out, all of you.
I needed to find you, anyway. Quickly, if you don't mind.

lines.st: In the shadows, some few 'sculptures' come to life, slowly easing towards Skadi.

lines.x: She's very peculiar.
Quite, Sheetmetal. Such a person, I've never before seen.
Nor I.
So you know how to speak after all.
Who are you?
A wandering singer.
I know not what a wandering singer is.
Sheetmetal, do not speak to her.
Your face, unfamiliar. Your attire, strange. You are not one from here. What is your business?
I want to ask a question.
Your clothes, very new.
Have you seen my fellow person?
You have much strength. You are able to eat your fill.
She came here before I did.
Your case, very heavy.
An instrument is not a light thing.
The thing in your grasp, very uncanny.
I assume your harps should come more or less the same.
(Plucks strings)
Ears, pained. It is a weapon.
It is able to not be.
Sheetmetal, this person, uncanny. Do not heed her. To speak, very tiring.
Bench. Let go of him.
Bench... Do you mean this child?
I didn't intend anything for him. He took something of mine. Things like that are significant.
You came to rob something.
I'm only taking back what's mine. And looking for someone who can understand me when I talk.
*Whimper*...
Our child, you may not touch.
Our things, you may not rob.
Hm?
Our rules, may not be broken.
Sheetmetal, you have gotten nearer. What are you doing?
She is going to harm our child, steal what is ours, break our rules.
Is she not our sister? If she is, she too has a share.
She is surely not.
You don't understand me.
I'll go and ask someone else.
You may not leave.
One moment, Sheetmetal...
Ouch!
There—There is a hole here.
Woodframe's hole. You forgot.
Oh, it comes in handy after all.
Those from outside, to our place, you should not have come!

lines.st: Skadi watches as the locals fall unconscious, and she picks up the suitcase she'd laid for a second on the floor.

lines.x: ...Gr—Greetings.
Hm? There's another one.
Don't—Don't hit me... I'm not very much like them. I know you're good.
You can understand what I mean.
You only came here following Bench.
*Whimper*... Woodframe, Woodframe...
He ran, I followed.
You bear no ill will towards us.
All I ask is he gives what's mine back.
Huh?
B—Bench... what did you take?
All shiny... all shiny... hard... it hurts... *whimper*...
That's not for you to eat! Pull it out of your mouth, quick!
I'm sorry, Bench tends to just grab at things he's never seen before. He doesn't understand these are things he shouldn't grab.
(Receives necklace)
Ugh, it's covered in spit... It's gotten your hand dirty.
It's fine, as long as I have it back.
We don't often see unfamiliar faces here. Did you come from the outside?
I came from a city elsewhere in Iberia.
It's very rare I see people from other places.
Your clothes... are very strange too. I've never seen material like this before. Does everyone from the outside dress that way?
Only wandering singers would.
Wandering... singers? I overheard you talking to Sheetmetal, but I don't know what that is.
Like me, they travel everywhere, occasionally stopping over, playing the harp, singing a song.
What about the case? What's inside it?
A saxophone.
What's a saxophone?
An instrument.
Are you really a wandering singer?
Mm.
(Quiet mutter)
So what did you come to do, then? Was it sing?
I came to find someone.
Who? Also a wandering singer?
Yes. She is a fellow of mine. We are colleagues.
Did she come to Sal Viento too?
Correct. Have you seen her?
You haven't told me what she looks like yet... oh, well! I said it just before, but you're the first time I've ever seen anyone like you.
I'll go and ask others. Can you guide me?
Guide? That trap... was my making. I thought you would've known.
I don't care.
You don't fault me? I thought you would at least have... reproached me some.
Let's go. I'm in a hurry.
Alright...
Agh!
Why—Why did you suddenly stop...? I knocked against your case. Ow...
I forgot to say. What's in my case is useless to you.
Apart from this, anything you might want, I can give you once you help me find her.
Er, I was only curious... Was I that apparent?
As plain to see as the trap you made.
Wow... singer, you not only have a pair of nimble hands, but a pair of formidable eyes too.
Ninety-eight...
Ninety-nine...
How do I ask questions here?
Huh? How do you usually ask them on the outside?
(With a thump, she sets down the case she was carrying.)
With this.
Um? The saxo... saxophone? You use instruments to ask questions?
Yes.
Slam slam, boom, kapow.
The sounds this instrument makes are quite bizarre.
It's very effective. They all usually open up straight away.
If they still don't talk, I can change angle, and go for another round.
Then I have a feeling that won't get you anywhere here.
Possibly.
(Strokes case)
In that case, the two just now. They already got talking.
Sheetmetal? They shouldn't even know of musical instruments. The people here usually keep themselves to themselves, and don't pay attention to anyone.
And they, er, talk very infrequently. Even if they knew, they wouldn't understand how to reply.
You've paid attention to me.
Probably because I'm a little odd. I'm not too like everyone else.
You talk a lot better than they do too.
Do I really? I'm so happy. It's all what Grandma Petra taught me.
Oh, if you want to find someone else to ask, I can bring you to Grandma Petra. She's the oldest one of us all here, and knows about the most too.
Alright.
Come, singer, let us walk through here.
Here?
Ninety-nine, ninety-nine...
Ninety-nine.
Do you mean them? You can ignore them, they're just standing around. They make it a bit of a squeeze, but it'll be alright even if you bump into them. They won't react, really. Will you, Tableleg?
Wha? She jumped—clear over their heads? H—How did she manage that? She didn't so much as touch anyone beneath her.
Do all people from the outside walk about like this? How magical!
Made it!
Grandma Petra! Graaaandmaaaa Petraaaa!
Don't shout! Are you trying to shake my ears apart?
Hee-hee, grandma, you're so very lively today! You're still—er, are you basking in the sun?
The sun... keh, where do you see the sun? I've been waiting all these years and I still haven't seen the sun. Anita, what are you getting up to? You're always scrambling about, doing this and that.
Around here, could anything important even happen?
Grandma Petra, I'm fine as I am. Someone else wanted to find you.
—Huh, where'd she go?

lines.st: Skadi stares fixed at the alley's other end.
There's no one to be seen there. At least, it seems so.

lines.x: How did you get over here all of a sudden?
Someone's watching.
That so? Where? I can't see anyone around.
They're hiding very well. Not well enough, is all.
Why are you so grim? Aren't you a wandering singer? Shouldn't you be very used to people staring if you perform?
...You're right.
Then let's make our way over! Grandma Petra's in alright spirits. You might really be able to ask something or other.

lines.st: At a street corner, an old lady rounds a pillar, turning circle after circle.
Her body trembles and shakes, yet her gait is no hobble.

lines.x: Red sky at night, set aglow by my fiery clothes... beloved one, o, take me to fly o'er the deep blue ocean...
Grandma Petra, what are you doing now?
Anita, oh, curse you, Anita! Did you not see I was dancing?
When a young girl is dancing, nobody can disturb her. Are you going to dance along with me, Anita?
I... I don't know how.
Spin a little slower. Watch you don't make yourself dizzy again.
Nonsense! How would I go dizzy? I could round it ten and some times, and the whole city would clap for me!
I... *cough* *cough* *cough*...
Take a little break. Look, we have a wandering singer here. She has a question to ask you.
A wandering singer? I've never seen any wandering singer in my good long years.
This sunlight... this sunlight's carried on far too long. Why hasn't night come yet? Once the sun's turned in, there'll be people playing away in the bar... Will you be playing in there too?
We dance in the square, yes, right here we do... oh, I could spin more times than you could count. I have a red skirt, girlie, and I must say, it's much more beautiful than the one you're wearing.
So many youngsters want to dance with me. I like Manuel the most out of all of them. He was the most handsome when he was young.
Yes, Grandma Petra, you've told me this. You've told me plenty of times now.
Besides, who's looking for me?
Is it you? Your red dress is so pretty.
Red... no, I don't want... I don't want one red.
The seawater! The seawater is alive!
Oh, no, here it goes again...
...suddenly came washing up. Everyone dead! All so fine... so many fine people. The survivors had nothing either to eat.
Manuel... Manuel told me he'd take me with him. Then he went back on that. He said all those people who left had never come back. All of them had died on the outside.
You've told me this before as well.
And then he said he'd protect me. He promised me—he said he'd protect me! But he only wanted to make a fool of me—
He wanted to swindle me out of all the food I'd kept—*cough* *cough*, I pretended to agree, and I took the silver vase my mother left behind—*cough* *cough* *cough* *cough*—
Red... I like red... no, I don't want...
Grandma Petra, Grandma Petra! Just leave it, okay? You always get out of breath once you start going on about this.
The one I brought to see you today wanted to ask about something else.
Ask? Who's asking?
Her. A wandering singer from the outside.
Tell her who you're looking for, quickly! While Grandma Petra still has the strength to answer.
Hello—
Who're you?
A wandering singer. I'm searching for someone just like me, come from the outside as well.
An out—An outsider! You're pulling my leg!
No outsider would ever come around these parts, they stopped caring about us long ago...
You came to steal something, didn't you? You're going to steal my food, my clothes, you're going to sup on my flesh, drink my blood—
Monster! You're a monster!
(Frowns)
Grandma, there isn't a monster. She's just a wandering singer.
Red hem... red... I've found one, here... the singing has arisen... Manuel is waiting for me, I must go, I should go...
Beloved one, o, set aglow by my fiery clothes... fiery clothes...
*Sigh*.
I'm sorry, Grandma Petra's particularly worked up today. Maybe I shouldn't have brought you to see her.
I'll take her back inside, singer. Give me a moment.
Singer, are you still looking for the one watching you?

lines.st: Across the opposite corner, a dark shadow stows away in an even deeper cloud's gloom.

lines.x: I'm fine.
Lurking trouble is like a beastling too scared to bare its fangs. No need to care.
Grandma Petra was...
She knows of playing instruments, and knows to dance. The way she spoke was akin to an outsider.
Really? No wonder. She speaks of so much, I can't make heads or tails of it.
She called me a monster.
She didn't mean to. Grandma often sees things that aren't real. It's like she's dreaming all the time.
Who isn't.
She's taken ill. She can't answer your question now.
A shame.
Don't be worried.
There are other brothers of mine where I live. I can take you to ask them in a while. I'm just not sure if they'll be willing to open up. Sometimes it really is so hard to speak with them.
Brothers?
Yes, brothers. You're an Iberian too. Don't you call them so?
My mother had no other children—but—I have very many brothers and sisters. In this town, we are all brothers and sisters. That's what the laws say.
And you believe in those.
What else would we do? When I was very young, Grandma Petra even told me that in those dark days past, we had made mistakes.
In those times, each one of us only looked after ourselves, hiding in each home, never speaking. Sometimes a can would be dug out, and we would, um, fight over it.
Then things gradually got better. Now we'll live together, and occasionally talk. Everyone's started to, erm—learn to share.
—'We want to survive, so we must love one another even more. For love is the inseparable bond between us. We may not steal, we may not fight.'
'Make oneself sturdy, but our whole self sturdier still. Only this way will bring a better life.'
This sounds familiar.
Really? The priest says everyone from the outside believes this too.
I forgot to say, the priest taught us this!

He'll tell us so much every time... most of it is lost on me. Apart from those few words just now, all I remember is we need to treat each other as brothers, and be a little kinder to each other. I suppose it is nice advice, isn't it?
Priest?
Yes. Just like you, he came from the outside too. Though that was very many years ago.
It was ever since he came that things improved. He's a brother among us.
Is he alone?
Most of the time he's alone. Oh, I heard from Sheetmetal's friends there was one more too, later on.
Who?
I've never seen her. She doesn't seem to show herself. Occasionally when the priest is speaking to us, she'll be standing behind him. I only ever see that one glimpse of her from far away.
Alright.
Why are you... in such a hurry suddenly? Do you need to go somewhere?
I'm going to the chapel.
Do you know how to get there?
Your chapel isn't that hard to recognize.
The way there's not so easy. You need to follow the coast from here. Come, let me show you.

lines.st: The girl leads Skadi along a lengthy stretch of path.
The two gradually near the shore, the chapel roof always somehow in their sight, yet still far away as ever.

lines.x: Why did you stop?
So they followed.
What do you mean? You've lost me again.
That clingy beastling.
That's strange. You keep saying beastling—that should be an animal, right? We've got no animals living here—they disappeared long ago!
It's fine. Let's keep going.
Very well, friend from the outside. You're in charge.
What did you see? It's right there.
But we don't usually go to the chapel at this time.
I have ways in.
You can enter, we all can. The chapel's entrance is forever open to the devout—the priest said so. But... you wanted to seek someone out, didn't you?
He isn't usually in the chapel at this time.
What times is he in?
Every so often, he'll appear before us of his own accord, and make friendly conversation with every one of us.
If you have any issues, those are also times you can go find him. No matter what it is that bothers you, he'll never reject you.
On such days, until the sun rises the next morning, he'll be in the chapel waiting.
That's right, he can also help you in your search! I'm sure the priest would know much more than any of us!
How long is 'every so often?'
Um, I haven't seen him in the recent while. I'm not like them—I don't really count the days. But it should be the next two days? All our food is about to run bare.
Alright.
Strange. Whenever you say alright, I always feel like it isn't a sincere reply.
No, that's wrong of me. The priest said, we shouldn't doubt others. Ugh, they all say I'm so fond of thinking of all these questions, and it won't help me lead a good life in any way.
Were those also his words?
Haha, he'd never tell me this. I'd slip off before he knew it.
It's Sheetmetal and his lot, they all hate me making a racket. And it's not like I'm always itching to say so much. The people here are all too quiet.
Whenever they won't talk, I get so bored, and the only thing I can do is talk to myself for hours on end.
It used to be that only Grandma Petra would talk with me. And now you too, thank goodness. Oh, no. Do you think I'm annoying as well?
Where I come from, there are many who love speaking more than you do.
It sounds like a wonderful place!
...It's okay enough.
Where you come from, do the people dance? Just like Grandma Petra was mentioning, dancing in the square?
Occasionally.
Then why haven't I seen you dancing? I've noticed your harp.
Grandma Petra's told me. Don't you all dance around while you sing?
My dance... none of you may see.
Why?
The time, the place, and those before me are all not right.
You're so strange, practically stranger than me. Just where is your home? Are all the people where you're from as strange as you?
(Plucks strings)
Where I'm from, there is no home.
And I do not know where my home is. My home is long gone.
Well, that's okay. My home was long gone as well, years ago in a gale, when the building collapsed. My mamá was inside when it happened, and she's gone too.
But that's okay. Everyone's in the same boat. If your house is gone, find another empty one and move in. Plenty of buildings fit the bill here.
That's right, if you're not happy dancing, then can you sing for me?
I've never heard anyone else sing before.
Maybe.
Wonderful!
Oh, visiting so many places has gotten me hungry.
You're hungry too, aren't you? If you're hungry, you can't sing, right?
Let's go find something to eat.
Um...
Am I running too fast? I forgot you wouldn't know the way.
Right, I'm Anita! You can just shout my name from now on, and that way you won't be left behind. Call for me, and I'll stop and wait for you.
Look at you, even now irresolute, an ocean fish lost in its course.
(She glances at him.)
You wish to know where I have brought you. You very much mind. You have not the qualification to take part in our congregation, yet your eyes tell me, you wish to know just what we will do to her.
Did you recover the kindred love between you? Could your heart be torn apart by what happened to your old relation? Do you feel it is unjust? Are you thus angered?
You are attempting to find what you cannot see.
But of course. That is the expected response.
You believe I wish to harm her. You are wrong. I wish to help her.
Only by elucidating where her—where all your pain lies at its source, can I better assist you in excising the problem point, is that not so?

As with you. You voluntarily became one of us, wishing to melt into the ocean true. Once you have completed this mission, your sordid nature will achieve purification. This is the ocean's charity to you.
And in the end, charity alike will be extended to all your old relations. So long as they alike can relinquish their narrow views of a past enmity.
To reunite you, that is the first step. To fathom you, that is the second step. And come the end, you will fathom us too.
If you are able to reduce the nonsense by a measure, we may cooperate so much more harmoniously.
I can also speak of things that will make you happy. Your old relation—the lucid one, she is already here.
Skadi?
She is not here.
Naturally, she is not. Without my invitation, none may enter this place.
She's entered the city. You know that.
You have expressed surprise. That is very interesting. There is no sound in this city that I do not hear.
And He admires all I do, has granted me the power, given my life further reach. My perception, my wit, all are no longer bound by the flesh you see before you.
I am approaching perfection. I will accept your grudges.
Hmph.
As for your old relation, now—her arrival was sooner than anticipated.
'Abyssal Hunters are bound by blood.'
She has no inkling of what awaits her. All you had to do was take the other away, and she has given chase here, not having once rested.

Truly a camaraderie to make one gasp. Does she know the state of you nowadays? What will she do when she confronts a traitor? Will she trust in your blood binding, old comrades rekindled, or will a blade pierce true through your body?
I have great expectations.
(She turns to leave.)
You needn't hurry to find her. You've done very well. You have brought this one back to where she should remain, and led another into the city.
He will be very pleased.
He?
You need not know.
We simply wait now. We wait until the time comes, and naturally, you may find your old relation then, and pass on to her our invitation.
She is not sure to come.
She will come. She will come seeking me herself.
You're back again!
We live in the best building in the whole north area. Look, this door still closes. A door that shuts closed is one of your best friends on a rainy day.
People are nearby. Two of them.
They're, well, they're usually here.
One-hundred-and-thirty, one-hundred-and-thirty-one, one-hundred-and-thirty-two...
Hi, Brick, are you doing good today?
One, two, three...
I'm sorry!
Poor guy, now he has to start over again. He's always sitting here, counting all the bricks in the street. Don't tell him I said, but each day's count isn't much like the last.

And also, that one's Stonecolumn. He likes making his way all around the street pillars. When it's all dark and rainy he doesn't come out. He's a little old, and his legs start hurting when he gets wet.
You can just ignore them, and they'll ignore you too, just like they ignore me.
Come in, singer. Let me give you an official introduction. In here is my home.
Hi, Sheetmetal. Hi, Drywall. You're awake!
(Strokes case)
Ouch...
Hey, Woodframe. You brought this outside person into the house again.
She's not bad.
She's from the outside.
Bench has taken a decent liking to her, haven't you, Bench?
Wgh... aogh...
Don't—Don't bite! You're going to chew the singer's skirt apart!
*cough*.
...Not that it'll come apart.
It's all slobbery again. That's not very pretty.
Aogh, au-auwgh...
This rascal really has taken a shining to you, singer.
You see? Bench wants her to stay too.
You are scheming something. Do not think, I do not know.
Er...
This will not, end well.
I'm not, okay, I don't have any plans at all.
What is the phrase...? Suit yourself.
The rules, may not be broken.
Whether by you, or by her.
I will be watching.
We shall go.
Phew... they finally left.
Singer, don't be afraid. This is just the kind of person Sheetmetal is. He may seem very frightful, but he won't really do anything to us.
Oh?
Okay, I know you're not afraid. Since you're so much more powerful than them.
I just wanted to say, if he really tried something—*snort*—I wouldn't be scared of him either.
Oh.
*Sigh*.
You're sighing.
This barrel's empty. So is the tray.
Let me just think... maybe there's still some food under the bed.
Is this the bed?
It is. Does it seem very odd? Were the beds in your home not metal?
I don't mind too much.

You can find wooden beds in some houses, too. Wood's surprisingly useful. Wood can burn, and make houses brighter and warmer at once. There are some times if you fall ill here when the winters won't be kind to you.
Eh-heh—look, this is my treasure box. I filled it a touch full when I woke up this morning.
Help me for a moment here, get this dry seagrass out of my sight. No, don't throw it on the floor. Even if we can't eat it, we can still pad out the bed with it.
You have a lot.
That's right. I hide everything useful inside here.
Take a look at this, they're clothes I'm sewing right now. I sharpened up a shell into a needle recently, and it's so much better to use than all those years-old ones.
I'm fairly short of material. Still need to go searching properly through the city south. Maybe there'll be some buried so deep that nobody's taken it yet.
It'll be getting cold soon, so I need to hurry up.
No, don't you dare, Bench, you can't touch these clothes! These are for Grandma Petra. Her cough just keeps getting worse.
Not this either! You let go!
Woodframe.
I've told you how many times? It's not Woodframe, it's Pictureframe!
Also, you can't even eat this! There's no point in you even having it!
Woodframe... Pictureframe?
You heard them calling me that?
I only know you as Anita.
I picked Anita for myself... I heard it in one of Grandma Petra's stories. She was a girl just like you, she could really sing. It sounds much nicer than Woodframe, doesn't it?
Most of us here have no names. Whatever came to mind first is what we use. Brick's always counting bricks, Sheetmetal has a sheetmetal fence running all around his house.
They call me Woodframe. Because underneath this pictureframe is where Grandma Petra found me.
You mentioned your own mother before.
She didn't give me up to anyone else. Brave, she was. When I was born, there were no children here. Children couldn't survive.
Those years, nobody dared go against Grandma Petra... she gave me a share of food. Don't let her poor health now fool you, she's this city's most fearsome person by far.
Oh?
Even Sheetmetal fears her.
Oh.
Things are much better now. That priest taught us how to survive. Just look at Bench, and those children over there. I believe in this place of ours becoming a lively one someday.
Aaah... ah...
So long as you don't keep taking what's mine. You're a good kid still, aren't you, Bench?
It doesn't matter if anything else gets taken away. Every night when I go to sleep, I hide this box away. They wouldn't know how to find it.
Ugh, we don't really go 'yours' or 'mine' here. I understand hiding things is bad. Singer, you won't go telling anyone else, will you? You're not interested in my things to boot.
Do you trust in me?
You're a guest. Grandma Petra taught me that we should properly receive guests.
There it is, I knew it was there. Look, there's still one fin left.
This odor...
It's half a month old. Still quite fresh as fish go.
Pull it apart, and this half... is for Bench. Here, Bench, food for you here!
Ah... aaah... (He swallows it.)
Alright, and this half is for you. Leave the bones when you're done eating. I can boil some soup with them after.
I can go to find food too.
But the day still hasn't come! If you went out now, you probably couldn't find anything to eat.
Can't say until you try.
Huh?
She talks, and suddenly she's gone. Seriously, how odd she is!

lines.st: The wandering singer in the red dress, carrying her tremendous case on her back, strolls casually through the street.
Still no one heeds her. In a street still and ashen, naught moves but a flutter of red.

lines.x: Still following.

lines.st: Skadi suddenly picks up in speed.
The indistinct shadow sweeps through the way, in the manner of a wave driven on by a gale.
It is as if she is about to charge headlong into the ocean—and then comes to a sudden halt.

The singer in the red dress clutches her harp to her chest, using one hand to clamber a nomadic city's wreckage, her vision sweeping past the chapel towering on the high cliff, resting on the pitch-black sea surface.
A sea positively calm.
From a crag hanging over ten meters high, she leaps down.

lines.x: The shore. She has gone to the shore.
Yes, Sheetmetal, your thinking is correct. She wants to enter the sea. She intends to set into it.
The time, is not yet come.
The priest spoke so. The tide must flood and ebb one-hundred times, before we are permitted, to enter the sea.
Sheetmetal, we cannot come here. The shore, does not permit us to.
That is meaningless. We will, block her.
Block her? As if we could defeat her?
The priest spoke, we must wait one hundred times, before we may go. It is only, the ninety-ninth time now.
She will obtain nothing at all. The shore today, has nothing at all.
So what is she looking for?
Sheetmetal, it is bitterly cold here, I am hungry, we cannot come here, and you should not ask.
Is there anything in the sea?
I do not know.
Even if you knew, what then?
I do not know. Sheetmetal, what you say, I do not know any of. I do not understand.
Where is the furthest we have traveled, in the past?
The westernmost of the west. A long time ago. In those days, we were, no taller than this. We could still, collect food in the town.
Borderline. We do not often, say this word. We have seen, this city's, edge.
That was you, Sheetmetal, not we.
At that time, I was dizzy with hunger.
You said, you wanted to go further still. You wanted, to push your luck.
It was to try my luck.
Hmph. You never could, make any sense. The priest gave you such years of teaching. You never could learn.
Heh-heh, Sheetmetal, you make. Sense of me.
You said so, at that time too.
You have always trusted me. You let me take the can you kept. I traveled another day.
And then you came back.
Yes, I came back.
You ate nothing, for several days after. I thought, you wanted to starve yourself to death.
I thought of it. But what meaning, would starving myself have?
After all, we will all perish come not too long.
You are giving me a shock. You said this back then too. I do not know what you are talking about.
Afterward... the priest came. It was only when he came, that I felt a change. We all changed.
The priest, has a good life. He says we can have one. What he says is true.
It is true. Because... it is the truth. He let us see it.
Because of the priest, I survived. We all survived. That is, very good.
That line, no one can cross.
Is that line, the seawater line?
All lines. Just like the traps Woodframe makes. They stop, even outsiders.
We cannot exit either. That is for the best.
Sheetmetal... your leg, is going to meet the water!
Water... yes. I shall not cross.
Even if I crossed over... crossed over, there may well be nothing there at all.
Phew... Sheetmetal, you finally stopped walking. You scared me just now.
Do not be like that, asking so many questions. You are like Woodframe, when you always ask. She was always strange. The priest said too that something is wrong about her, did he not?
Questioning... cannot help us.
Only answers have any meaning.
You impress me so, Sheetmetal. You are the only one who remembers, the priest's words as strongly.
Look, she has not stopped walking along the shore.
Sheetmetal, where are you going? Are you going to follow too? No, that is not right, we may not, and cannot follow her!
Even I know, we cannot defeat her. We have no way to drive her out.
She cannot return now.
That... That is a very good thing for us.
I want the big case she has. There is surely much to eat inside.
The case is not here. She took it.
There is only this... this, she placed it on the rocks. I do not know what this is.
She is always holding it. It should be something good, no?
May I... May I try a bite, Sheetmetal? I am so very hungry.
(Plink-plunk)
What—What is this? I have never heard a sound like this before.
(Plink-plunk, plink-plunk)
Stop it quickly, Sheetmetal, this sound is making my stomach and teeth shake without end. If you are fond of holding it then hold it. I am going back after all.
No meaning. This also has no meaning.
This useless thing. It should be left on the ground. To rot.
The whole way, I thought it was somebody else following me.
You—You returned! How were you able? Nobody who goes to, the shore at this time, will return.
You want to enter the sea.
I'm just taking a walk around the area.
Is there anything in the sea?
There's nothing at all.
That cannot be.
You have turned stranger and stranger, Sheetmetal. You are still asking these questions I do not understand. Did we not come here to block her?
Now she is heading back. We may go back.
You saw it. You know.
Sheetmetal, you are shaking. Have you become ill?
Don't come near.
And what if I do? Will you beat us so we are lying on the floor, like before?
There is something in the sea, correct?
Don't move.
Aaaaagh—
Drywall!
This stone... her movements, are truly fast.
Was that a sound from my mouth? Sheetmetal, I made a sound. My heart is beating wild. Have I become ill too?
I believe, your head will explode with a bang. Just like a shell smashed open, there will be something shining bright white inside.
Will I die then?
We will know the next time we try. What has fallen apart now is something else. I have never seen a thing like this before.
Why can it... still move?!
Run. Head back to the city. Quickly.
—Remember to leave my harp here.
Ah, you're back! I was just about to go find you.
How come you've got sand stuck to you... did you go to the shore?
Yes.
I told you not to go! But you just don't listen.
Hurry, come inside with me, it's warm.
No need.
Fine. This is for you! I found something to eat. I gave those clothes I half-sewed to Uncle Fireplace, and took some things from his. He's got quite a lot of food left, so he shouldn't mind it.
It'll be forever before winter comes. If I just work at it a little more, I'm sure I'll find materials to use.
This shellmeat...
Just what is it you're eating?
Ow! It hurts when you grab me like that... you're much too strong!
What is it you're eating...?
We—We pick them off the coast!
There is no food in the sea here. Nothing to harvest whatsoever. This part of the ocean is silent.
I told you, if you went to the shore today, of course there'd be nothing to eat.
The time has to come...
What time?
One-hundred...
One-hundred!
Oh, no! I hadn't even paid attention to the count. We're already at a hundred, then.

lines.st: As if receiving some sort of signal, each 'busied' person scattered all across the street assembles together.
From all their mouths the same number comes, as they surround a pot, one after another remove something identical thing from it, and then silently leave.

lines.x: That pot.
Weren't you asking me where all the food came from?
On every hundredth flood and ebb of the tide, the grownups all get together like this, and each one takes a shell out from it.
The shells in the pot are mostly white ones, and only one is red.
Once the sun's gone down, the one who's taken the red one will go to the shore.
And on morning the next day, there'll be food all over the shore.
And the one who went?
Went to go live in the sea. Just as the laws say.
You believe in this too?
Well, where else do you think they go?
One-hundred. It has finally come.
Here, Woodframe, why that face? This is a good day!
Very soon, out of us, another lucky one will be chosen. They will leave before us, to live a better life.
Come tomorrow, we will even have, fresh food aplenty. We will all survive. No matter whether gone to the sea, or left on the shore.
You do not need to worry. You are already old enough. Very soon you will join this too, and have this greatest, opportunity.
For now, you may go on as before. Give your blessings to that sibling.
Sheetmetal, I, I...
You have fallen over. Truly hopeless. What happened at the shore, earlier, weakened your leg.
I do not understand why. I, I am so hungry... I have no strength. I am ill... I am going to die.
That is right, what about the shell? The one I just drew...
You dropped it on the ground.
Is it... Is it a red one?
Can, I go to live in the sea?
In the sea, I will not hunger. Is that not right, Sheetmetal? I will live, a good life.
You will... erk, bless me too, as the priest told, will you not?
You have mistaken it.
Take it, for this is your shell. Look carefully at, what color it is. Do not let the saltwater muddle your eyes.
A white one? Why would it be white... did I see wrong? I could not have. My eyes, are very fine.
(Plucks strings)
You are here too.
So this, is how you use that thing.
*cough*, Sheetmetal, the shell in your hand... your hand, is clutched so tight. It is bleeding, is it not? I can see, reddest red...
If it's blessings you want, I have none for you.
You understand nothing.
This is what is truly meaningful. I know, at least, where I should go. I will very soon learn.
What are you all doing?!
That outfit... it's an Inquisitor!
What is an Inquisitor doing here all of a sudden? They come so rarely.
It was so many years ago when an Inquisitor last came... I was still very young then, and we were all living miserably.
We couldn't find any more food in the city, and so many of us were ill. Everything was a mess, and everyday people passed away.
The Inquisitors took a few people away. Those people never came back.
Since then, the days have gotten a little better. We still starve, or die of illness, but at least we won't, um, we won't...
I mean... that's an Inquisitor.
Oh.
Shh, singer, don't anger her. She doesn't seem very happy at all.
I've seen what's happening here, and I've even heard it. You're plotting to send someone into the sea... Do you not know what it is you're trying to do?!
Speak! Why won't you speak? I don't like it, I don't like anything about this place. Disease, famine... I thought I'd have seen it here, with how hard it is to escape from.
According to prior reports, none of you should even be alive at this point, after all these years!
Yet reality begs to differ. You all seem to me in better shape than I'd imagined, yet blighted all too fiercely.
Do you all understand what your actions will eventually cause?
Look at me, answer me! Don't you know who I am?
You are an Inquisitor.
Thus, you are required to answer me, citizen!
This is the ocean's choice. The one who is chosen is the one who accepts it.
This isn't right! What in the world is the ocean's choice? As if the ocean had some kind of will of its own... a truly unsound suggestion!
The way I see it, you are conducting a collective murder! How could anyone entering the sea survive? On... on what basis do you sentence the innocent?
Entering the sea, is not death.
How do you know?
Do you know?
I... That's irrelevant. I only know that their departure will in all likelihood be more terrible than death itself.
And right now, I witness that you are coercing one amongst you.
We are not making them do it. This is their own choice.
If they want to go to the shore, we should not obstruct them. The laws do not say they cannot do this.
Unbridled nonsense! This is not in any way proper!
Then, what is proper? You are an Inquisitor. You may indicate to us.
At the very least, I know more about wrong than you all seem to.
What you're carrying out is impermissible conduct. Whether you see it or not, I still smell an odor of wickedness—
(Draws sword)
It seems you have no fear of my sword. Perhaps, though, you truly don't consider this a homicide.
You fear not even death. You're all becoming monsters before my very eyes.
Or yet... I suppose there's someone who wants you turned into monsters.
It may be your insanity was tugged astray by some actor. I will unearth this sin's source, I swear!
I'm out of here.
Hm?
She's the one who can't hold it in. It's nothing to do with me.
Who... Who's she? Do you mean... the Inquisitor?
Let's at least let her try.
You've looked in an awful lot of houses this whole way.
You don't need to follow me.
Can you really find who you're looking for, turning through one building after another like this?
I'll keep looking, then.
Traveling the city north to south, west to east, would take a whole day or two.
If you didn't follow me, I could move faster.
Does the one who wanted you here know you're looking for her? Didn't she mention any specific place?
Ugh, it'll be dark in just a bit, so let's rest up. Singer, have you readied anywhere to stay tonight?
Anywhere will do.
You can head back with me. I've mentioned the buildings in the north are a little better for wear. Plenty of us all live together.
Look, there was still a little fin oil left over from last time. Uncle Fireplace was up early to light the lamps, and now the room's nice and bright.
I'll be fine.

lines.st: Skadi randomly kicks open a roadside door. The door belongs to a half-collapsed building.

lines.x: I'll stay in here.
This one? This one hasn't had anyone in it for forever.
I don't care about that.
Never mind a bed, you'd barely find a single even nook in there. If you can't lie down, how are you going to sleep?
Standing, sitting. It makes no difference.
Incredible...
You're so strange. I didn't know what wandering singers were like, but the feeling you give me isn't like them.
I thought people who love singing would all love laughing, joking. Not like you, you barely talk.
And would people who love dancing not mind sitting somewhere dust-caked like you are, getting your skirt all dirty?
You should go home.
Are you annoyed with me now?
This is no good place. As you said.
But I want to keep you company.
I don't need company.
Ugh, I realized. You've stared outside the whole time you've been here. Where are you looking at? The shore, the chapel?
Are you going to head out again at night? Say, climb the hill, and take a look inside the chapel?
That is none of your business.
True, it isn't. I'm just curious.
Take it.
Your harp? What are you giving me this for?
You've been eyeing it the whole time. If you want it, just take it.
What a pretty harp.
(Plink-plunk)
Such a nice sound. But I have no clue what to do with it. I can only watch you use it.
Then what do you want?
Do you feel as though I'm only following you because I want what's yours?
I just want to follow you, really.
I know the only thing on your mind is finding someone, finding that fellow one of yours.
I need to verify a few things.
You've been searching for her for many years now, haven't you?
Hm?

That's the feeling you give me. The moment I mention finding her, the look in your eyes is different. They're the eyes of someone who's been on so very many journeys. There's times when the look in Grandma Petra's eyes is the same.
So wonderful. I think, in the end, you can free yourself from her.
What?
Burdens like this are too heavy.
I don't understand what you mean.
No matter how long you persist for, come twenty or thirty years' time, whether it's you or her, you'll only want to be freed.
And no one can persist for that long of a time, besides.
What are you saying here?
I'm saying, you might just be the kind who's fated exceptionally well. You don't have to search for her anymore.
Here, only those without any burden can live for very long. If their minds are fixated on one thing for so long, they go mad.
Don't take me just for someone young. I've seen a lot of people go mad.
You don't understand.
I don't understand what happened between you in the past. But things of the past, good or bad, can crush people once too much amasses.
Just like I'm unsure if Grandma Petra's illness has gotten worse, every time she brings up the name Manuel.
You're still young. You don't need to live such a hard life.
Things aren't as simple as that.
Simple or not, you can try talking about it all the same.
I'll be right here. I want to hear.
They're...
Even if I don't pay them mind, they won't just leave.
The more I don't pay them mind, the more they'll come find you, drown you...
Here, you drink some too.
(She tilts her head and gulps.)
Stop—not too much in one go, leave a drop for me. I need some too to help with sleepless nights.
Goddamn. You piece of work, you.
My ass, you're the disaster. You got Juan killed... This damnable papá couldn't even give you an earful in time before you came back looking half-dead. What the hell can I even say to you?
I can't. A tomar por culo. I can't deal with it.
...I'm sorry.
The enemy's aim was me. No one who nears me... has a happy end.
Look at what you're saying. Cursing me, aren't you?
You should keep your distance from me too.
That I can do. Don't have to ask me twice.

All right, sichtír, that doesn't mean I believe you're some ill omen. This line of work's bloodthirsty, and that little idiot didn't listen to his papá, died, and died in some goddamn mystery asshole's hands. If even you couldn't do anything, did I ever have a chance?
That's not a hatred I can avenge. I've got nothing I can do to you either.
Once we're done with this bit of drink, we'll be off again, going wherever we go.
Alright.
You bastard! Were you always like this? Can't even find a single kind word, not so much as a profanity, huh?!
I've never been too good at the language here. I'm still not.
Nah. You're just not willing to admit you've made a friend.
Dammit. If we're to part, you might as well spare a couple more words. Where are you heading next?
I don't know.

Same as ever, then. Drifting here and there, taking on missions with no point, fighting wave after wave of enemies who couldn't even take you if you were using one finger. Like living in an unending dream, isn't that?
Skadi tilts back and swigs. But the alcohol seems without a shred of effect on her.
Then where can I go, in your opinion?
You're all bounty hunters in order to make money, to live.
But I long since had nothing left.
It's savage, orderless. There's not even an automatic toilet. I don't want to be here, and this place doesn't need me. Why should I be here?
Weak, ignorant. Both plainly insatiable yet scared of dying. The things don't even understand me, yet when they come looking for me, they're not even willing to get out of the way.
I can't figure it out.
Hold it, did you just crush a few bottles?
Beheading prey. Disposing of enemies. I'm born to do this.
They all die, and I live on. What do I live on for? Why do these disasters always come calling, but never kill me... always the ones by my side?
Juan was a good person.
I don't need you to tell me, you damned Ægir hag!
Then why? Why is it always them who die?
I await, I search, I make sure all those shadows pursuing me will be slain, but I can't even find them, I can't even catch sight of them.
So what am I meant to do? What can I even do, except keep far away from everyone?
Who can even... tell me at this point?
Fine, fine. You win. Tah-hathek.
This whole bottle's yours. Down it, give crying your eyes out a go, like me. Then get some proper sleep.
It doesn't do much. I've already tried.
Go dream actual dreams, hunter. For you, dreams might just be the more real thing. You never know, maybe you'll find some clues in there—you know, like a, uh, treasure map that only belongs to you?
Find it. And then you can be on your way all the better. Cover your head and move forward. It beats being stuck circling in place.
A treasure map.
Singer? Are you okay?
You seemed very pained for a moment... I didn't know you could make that sort of face.
It's fine.
And then you started pinching this thing. If you didn't treasure it so, I would've believed it was some awful artifact that angered you so much you wanted to pulverize it.
You don't... look angry at all.
This is hers.
Her? The fellow person you're looking for? Fine, I can see you just won't give up.
...She's not a burden.
She's the... one who helped me. She revealed the course of a craft to me.
If I keep following its direction and sailing on, maybe I'll be able to find answers.
Singer, what are you looking for?
I still don't know. It may be who I am. It may be what I should do.
When... When you're searching for things in the ruins, do you know what you're possibly going to find?
Wow. Put like that, I actually don't know.
I think, once I've found it I'll know.
Let me in!
Shoot.
Singer, don't you cross words with her. Open the door.
Why?
You're not an Iberian.
Say what you will.
That's an Inquisitor. Hmm...
I know you're in there! Don't get cocky about your disguise, I saw you the moment you entered the city!
Open the door, citizen. You don't know what kind of person you're sheltering...
Sheltering? No, I, I didn't mean to hide her...
I'll give you a count of three. If you don't open up, don't blame me for being discourteous.
One, two—
Three! *cough*, *cough* *cough*, so much dust! How long has this place been uninhabited...
Aha, Ægir, I've found you!

lines.st: The Inquisitor's blade is keen and swift, the air itself practically sundered in two.
The one meant for the blade's downswing turns her waist, sliding to the side in one unfathomable motion.
She keeps close with the blade's point, and it follows her unerringly, yet it cannot split even one of her hairs.

lines.x: Wh—What fast moves—
Singer... did you dodge?! That posture... I can't imagine it myself.
Stood on the chair... the backrest? People can stand on that? How is she stood so steadily?!
A fluke and no more!
I must see for sure, how long can your good luck last you?
She dodged again! In a single swish—she went from this end of the house to that... She's even lighter than seagrass flung in a gale, even faster!
That stick of yours is useless against me.
St... Stick?!
This is an affront...
You will pay for your false words!
Whoaaa—so strong too! The chair split apart, the cupboard, and now there's even a crack through the floor!
I—I can't bear to watch... oh, the singer...
Wait, she's alright? She evaded all of that? Such nimbleness!
No... it doesn't seem like she's dodging. Her steps aren't forced in any way. It actually has a peculiar feel of rhythm to it...
So—So beautiful.

lines.st: Sweat drips from the young Inquisitor's flushed face. Her blade hand is still steady, but her breaths are coming faster.
The narrow room is cut to utter pieces. To Skadi, this is no different to her slow ambles across the open barrens.

lines.x: How long do you plan to hide, Ægir?
Like the rest of your kind, you steal into our country, stowing away in the shadows—
Heyah!
Did that slant away? The slash cut against the wall...?
Has the Inquisitor... tired out?
Heh! At no moment does my sword ever slant—
The—The wall collapsed!
How fortunate, we're on the other side...
But the singer... has no way out now. The Inquisitor did that intentionally.
Nowhere left to hide.
Take this blow, for it is your crimes put on trial!
Damn it.
Gh... how?
How can my blade be so casually blocked?
Though I have not used all my strength... person of Ægir, just what are you?
A wandering singer.
Drivel! I've been following you ever since you entered Sal Viento.
Who are you fooling? What wandering singer... has such skills?!
Seems Mister José wasted his effort.
And who is this José?
And your acts at the shore! I saw it all!

lines.st: The Inquisitor flurries her blade, hacking and slashing, thrusting, each blow coming quicker, more exact than the last.

She no longer simply charges the Ægir in her gaze, but relentlessly switches angles, blitzing slices through the surrounding space. If the enemy shows no openings, her swordhand will force the openings from them.
However, Skadi is open, head to toe. She has no intent of defending at all.
Every supple strike melts, one by one, into a traceless muffle.

lines.x: Confess! Why have you stolen into our land?
You care too much.
S—So strong!
She's not just unafraid of the Inquisitor, she's taunting her?
It might not be a taunt. The singer just speaks this way. I've gotten used to it after half the day. But... *cough*, now the Inquisitor is even angrier.
I am an... Inquisitor!
All things that occur in Iberia are my business.
All errors proscribed under the law... we find, we even out.
There's something not right about the sea here. Their numbers have grown. This city is on the eve of... no, perhaps it has already become a lair of evils.
Speak, Ægir. All these abnormalities, they are all your doing, correct?
It was you who brought about disaster, had those things assemble here—
Yes.
You inculcated heretical thought into the citizens here, drove them to enter the sea. Look at them, they've all now become as grotesque as you!
Hm?
You undoubtedly saw it. You clearly brought something about in the ocean here. Yet see the look in your eyes—
You simply—do not care!
Mm...
How can such indifference claim resemblance to a normal human? And you dare to say this wasn't your doing?!
I will not let you escape, Ægiii—iiir! Do you consider the calamity you've brought to this country a trifle? Your lot are scheming something, of this I am absolutely certain!
Huh?
What's the Inquisitor talking about?
The singer's only been here for half a day... and what disaster? Who's grotesque?
Don't we... voluntarily enter the sea?
What other Ægir are there?
I don't understand.
You can't overcome me.
How can we know without trying further?
This is a pure waste of time.
Tch. You're correct. What have I been wasting all this talk on you for?
I should have long since—apprehended you first.

lines.st: All of a sudden, the Inquisitor sheathes her blade. From her side she draws a handcannon, grips it in both hands, and aims at Skadi.

lines.x: Huh?!
Get down. Don't come out.
Heh, do you know my might now, Ægir?
(She brushes off her hem.)
An extra bullet hole. Can't return this to Mister José in original condition now.
You... You! You're without a scratch?! How could you be? My aim was true!
Are you finished?
Your weapon. You don't use it lightly.
Otherwise, you wouldn't be talking to me now. You ought to aim a second time.
I don't understand what you're talking about, nor do I care.
I don't want to...

lines.st: A beam of light pierces the dead of night, shone directly on Skadi's face.
At the far end of the road, from where the light came, appears a single figure carrying a lamp.
Skadi's face shows no expression, her impatience just now gone with it.
But in the instant she sees that not-so-bright light, the Inquisitor's face shines.

lines.x: High Inquisitor!
Stand down. You're no adversary for her.
But I...
Stand down.
By your order!

lines.st: Ten meters separate Skadi from the person before her, as they stare face to face.

lines.x: You. You're very strong.
You should not have set foot in this city.
I'm already here.
Violate the rules, and you must pay the price.
I can't care about that much.
You will remain here.
I don't want to fight with you.
Hmph. Wicked Ægir, you're surely frightened! Now that the High Inquisitor is here, you absolutely cannot run.
But, if this is necessary—
(Ægirian) I will take you down.

lines.st: Begin. Quicken. Clash.

lines.x: Such speed! So much faster than her prior hand-to-hand bout with me!
With speed like that, I could hardly even hope to touch her hemline... Just how much more was she still hiding?!

lines.st: Before she collides with the High Inquisitor, Skadi steps back.
As if a fish tossed in the air by the ocean waves, she leaps high, and lands on a dilapidated roof.

The High Inquisitor follows in mirror fashion. Their figures crash together, pull apart, then crash again. The city can barely endure such a clash, torn apart as it is, and fragments of buildings are building up rapidly on the street.

lines.x: Her attack and evasion are becoming more and more rapid, as are my instructor's. I almost can't keep up now.
Was I really... still this lacking?!
No, this won't do. That's an Ægir. Of course I cannot lose to an Ægir.
Yet this Ægir seems, somehow, to be using that case of hers as a weapon... Just what is that made of?! Why is it so tough?!
But my instructor isn't using the handcannon. If he was... wouldn't we have her seized in a blink? It'd blow this entire street into slag.
Is that... simply an Ægir, however? Her appearance is nothing at all like those monsters...
She's using... some form of Arts, is it? I can't quite tell. And our lamp... cannot inhibit her power whatsoever.
—This Ægir has too many secrets kept with her.

lines.st: The shaking throughout the whole street gradually ceases. Amidst the shards and smoke, the senior Inquisitor appears again.

lines.x: High Inquisitor! You're victorious. I knew no one could ever sway you by even a hair.
Er, where's that Ægir?
Fled.
What? That... how?
She is injured, and cannot run far.
I'll search for her—
You cannot overcome her.
Ahem... But we absolutely cannot allow her sort to run free along the coast!

Direction suggests she may have made for the citizens' assembly area. She's likely mixed in with the crowd, and has accomplices among the citizens aiding her. I'll search the houses for her one by one.
If you believe this is right, then go.
Understood—
High Inquisitor, was she the one who brought anomaly upon this city?
I had you go and see. What was it you saw?
The behavior of the locals is highly abnormal. It's as if they live every day in an unwoken state. They act, yet not to complete anything. When they eat, it's not even to fill their stomachs.
They're entering the sea, and they don't even consider it suicide.
Did you put a stop to them?
Yes. This is evidently wrong, after all.
What we are concerned with is the results. If you believe your corrections can have right results, then go do so.
Right results...
If they cannot, then you must make some choices.
Inquisitor—with your own two eyes, your blade, you must stare down the greatest menaces to Iberia.
Yes, sir!
The greatest menace to Iberia... is those monsters presently climbing ashore.
Could it be? These people who head into the ocean have some relation to these monsters?
Yes... they are surely related.
I remember you once taught that abnormality often takes more than a single form. Between all these terrible visions, there must be connections.
