To address an issue—you must first see clearly where that issue lies.
I—I know!
—It's those Ægirs hidden in the darkness, conspiring everything!
They're hidden ashore, concealed in the minor loopholes of our laws, rotting our cities' skin and flesh, destabilizing our fellows' wills, attempting to corrode our country's muscle and bone.
Before their plot prevails, before it drags this city into the abyss of despair—
We must—find them—and wipe them out!
Singer... singer? Are you in here?
I've been searching for you forever. The Inquisitors won't know about this place. There are too many empty houses here, and come night, searching will be awfully hard.
I know... I'm sure you want to avoid us. That the farther you stay from where we live, the better.
Finding you here definitely wasn't easy... phew... it's so close to the coast.
Is this the sound of the waves? I never knew it was so loud. And... I can hear breathing now.
Singer, what are you doing in here? Your breathing's become quite different.
You're all in danger.
What are you talking a... um, what's—are you hurt?
I'm bleeding. Get away!
Thinking of leaving? It won't be that easy.
The Inquisitor?! Did you follow me the whole way...? I—I didn't realize.
I could hardly let a commoner notice me. *cough* Of course, even if I don't tail you, I'd still have methods of finding this place!
You again.
What's wrong? Scared now?
Earlier—Ahem—I concede, I was negligent for a moment, and narrowly let you escape.
But now, Ægir, I'll definitely have you caught!
B—But, she's already hurt—
Hmm? Are you certain about getting in my way, so as to protect a dangerous Ægir, citizen? If so, you'll become a criminal, the same as her!
You and all the others are unalike. At the very least, your eyes show fear.
Until now, you have not yet done wrong. Out of the way! I don't want to have to sort you out with my blade.
It's not the time—
It's not what time? Are... Are you scorning me? You think I have absolutely no way to seize you?
Hmph. Certainly, I may not be the High Inquisitor. But I've already made my preparations.
I will deal with you, no matter the cost to me!

lines.st: Sword still unsheathed, Skadi proceeds to grasp the Inquisitor's arm—pulling her behind Skadi's own self.

lines.x: What—What is the meaning of this?
Don't make noise.
Unhand me, you Ægir reprobate! You'd better not think I can't employ my sword with a hand pinned, and I still have the handcannon—
Keep it down. They'll hear you.
Ngh-ghhh... I can't move... my other hand either...
Um, can either of you hear something?
I heard... sticky things, rubbing along the floor... so, so many... and the sound was right outside.
What... is that? It's not the work of any of us. I've never heard it before...
They're closing in.
Their numbers are growing.
What is causing it? The people of this place, or that woman and her companion?
Ten of them—
Twenty of them.
—Still growing, still advancing. Something is leading them to clamber ashore, and spill into the city.
And their course stems from—
The chapel.
Hm? They are very excited.
Are you sure you should be petting these things so?
Pay mind to the words you use. They are not 'things' of any sort. They are already a part of this expanse of ocean. They have become the heirs born of the sea, and are much purer than you are.
Ah... the news on the tide has already arrived. Tonight will be yet another special one.
They have surely smelt the scent. When bloodkin join them, they become so spirited, so agitated, that they even wish to emerge from the sea, and give their welcome on land.
It is very normal. We all love our own brothers and sisters.
Do you truly treat them as your own brothers?
Why not, I ask? At times of necessity, I am willing to address those of land, those who cannot help but still walk on two legs, as my brothers too.
If you did not hold the most dirtied, mixed blood within you, then one day, you may also possess this chance.
Oh? What a crying shame.
(He touches the seawater.)
Fascinating. This evening... they seem to be especially vigorous. Perhaps because so many old friends have come as guests to their home.
Are you not curious? Wondering just what is happening upon the surface.
...Loud.
So loud. I cannot sleep.
Stuff your head in your quilt.
I cannot hear.
I cannot see.
Ngh... So hungry...
So hungry.
So hungry.
Sheetmetal...
Ah!
Uwargh-wah-hh—
They're here.
What—What are these horrendous things?! Black... flowers? Or fish? Why do they move? How disgusting, eugh...
It couldn't be... That's not right, this doesn't make sense!
They're knocking on the windows! Are they... do they want to enter?!
W—We cannot let them!
Close the windows shut—
It's pointless. The windows here are all broken. Look at the top, it's full of cracks...
—They're reaching in!
Uwargh, it's still moving! Is this tiny chunk writhing on the floor trying to force its way to us?!
Even supposing it's one of those things... nothing gives it the right to be so difficult!
So very many... there's still so many outside.
Why can I not cut them down?!
My lamp... no, the lamp would be of no use against them.
I only have enough ammunition for one more round.
I—Inquisitor?!
Freeze! In a moment, I'll fire, and you two run for the interior. The ruins ought to be a little sturdier than these worn-out windows.
Your hand is trembling...
Holy scriptures... lend me strength.
I—I do not fear them!
I'll head out right away, kill as many as I can, and I should be able to hold a wave back. You must run with haste...
You, take her and hide inside.
What? What are you spouting?! Have you not seen? I can't cope with—this many! They're nigh coming in already. There's no difference between the inside and outside!
Remember, as far from doors and windows as possible.
You utterly don't listen to people when they tell you—
I'll go out. Draw them away.
What?!
Singer, you can't go outside either...
You're very strong, stronger than anyone I've seen before, but you're hurt! And these odd things—there's—there's so many of them!
Inquisitor, don't—don't leave either...
What if we all hide together? I—I know there's a cupboard in here, and the door still closes. It's in the back. If all three of us squeeze, we should hopefully...
I have to go out. Only this way, will you survive.
Hey! Ægir, even if you want to play the hero, this isn't your place to!
Listen well. This is genuinely nothing to do with you.
Because—they're here looking for me.
Skadi pushes open the door.
A long-lost scent following the sea breeze bores into the brain's tissue, creatures never of dry land pack densely into the streets.
They bubble up and down, spitting, contracting, imitating rot and growth in their actions, the shore of civilization falling to their sinister bodies.

lines.st: Skadi takes another step onward.
The streets have calmed.
The wandering singer closes the bulky box and carries it on her back again.
Skadi begins to step forward.
She takes one step and then another. The pile of limbs and coagulated fluids beneath her feet instantly rot and weather away.
On her third step, as the hems of her dress flutter in the sea breeze, the tiny particles of crystal scattered all across the ground disperse into the air as well.

lines.x: D-Did you see that just now?
I saw all of it.
How did she even do that?
She is a singer...
Inquisitor, this is nothing strange for her. That's something she's always been capable of.
Singer... what? You're still calling her by that name?
How else should I call her? You're the Inquisitor. You tell me.
I need to go outside.
Hey, wait!
What are you doing? So you think you can stop me too? I am an Inquisitor!
What is this... So slimy...
Phew... I-I got it! I landed a blow! That was close, Inquisitor.
Thank goodness you smashed this chair to pieces. It gave me a weapon when I needed one to smack this thing. It would've climbed on top of you otherwise!
Y-You...! All of you are out of your minds!
How could you not be scared, seeing these things?
I was. Just a little. They're not as scary as you, though.
You... How dare you compare me to these monsters?!
Fine, I won't argue with you. You haven't seen them before, and you have no idea what they are capable of. It's no wonder you are not scared.
Wait, the other inhabitants are coming outside. They... What are they doing?!
They're not scared at all either...
The problem...
I need to find where this problem lies...

lines.st: The problem, as the Inquisitor sees it, is the most abnormal sight she sees unfolding before her eyes.
The Inquisitor looks at the girl standing before her. Then, she looks over at the lone Ægir standing outside.
Finally, she turns her gaze to the inhabitants now lining into the streets.

lines.x: Sand.
Salt... So salty.
That thing just now. You can eat it. It's gone. A shame.
Too bad.
You can eat salt.
Yes, it's edible.
More. More... Mhmm, blurgh.
It's dirty.
Swallow it. More...
Stop right there!
I still have questions for you, and you will answer them—What do you mean, they're here for you?
I'm bleeding.
I can tell that much. You were wounded in your fight with the High Inquisitor, so you ran away. How's that got anything to do with these... monsters showing up?
They can smell my blood.
They're here to... kill you? I saw them all dashing toward you—
Perhaps.
Perhaps?
They can't hurt me.
Y-You! Do you know how annoying the way you talk is?
It's the truth.
They—I mean these monsters—They're gone.
Yes.
They're all gone without a trace, as if they never were. If I didn't see them clearly with my own eyes, I would've thought this all a dream.
These monsters... the Sea Terrors. I've come across them plenty of times before. They don't look that much like these ones, but they have that same disgusting stench.
But that was on the beach, and they never show up in such numbers. Do you mean to tell me they do not actually live in the sea?
They only show up when someone goes look for trouble at the shore, right? Just like what you were doing during the day.
And now they came this far away from the beach. They've nearly made it to the streets.
They can make it far more places than you can imagine. They can spread by changing form, permeating every corner of the world.
Be it iron-forged cities, villages built atop the highlands, or barrenlands with nothing but sand. Wherever one can go, they can too.
How is that possible?!

The world is surrounded by ocean. Just because you cannot see the ocean does not mean that the land where you stand will forever remain untouched by its waters. The seas are alive and ever flowing, and so are the calamities they bring with them.
I know there are places where the seas are dangerous, but I didn't know that the danger existed... in the lands outside Iberia too.
Danger exists everywhere.
Still, these calamities are here because of me. If you come any closer, you will be affected too.
You... Just who are you?!
I already told you.
Enough with the wandering singer thing! I saw it all.
You were fighting them, and you know exactly how to fight them too.
When you finished them off, your technique was so much better than when you were fighting the High Inquisitor and me.
I don't have quite the same handle on fighting humans.
A-Ahem... Let's dispense with the strange talk... I know you are a strange one, but still...
Were you... a hunter? I can kind of see that in your eyes.
If you think I am, then sure.
And you hunted these... Sea Terrors before?
They need to be eliminated.
Ugh... The way you talk just grates me so much, but—I don't even know why—coming from you, this doesn't sound like a lie at all.
Singer, hunter... It doesn't matter to me what you were.
Going by the circumstances, I suppose you saved not just my life, but everyone who lives on this street, maybe even the entire town.
You may be the enemy of my enemy, but that does not make you a friend.
No matter the case, I need to do my job. I have to take you away.
You were asking me what I am doing here.
I am here to seek an answer, and this answer only has to do with me... and the person I am looking for.
You wouldn't tell me a thing earlier.
You didn't give me a chance to tell you.
You may have given me an answer now, but that doesn't mean my judgment has changed.
You don't belong to this city. A person like you showing up is a mistake.
Not to mention... You told me yourself you brought this disaster with you.
—It's certainly not how I imagined it, though.
Nonetheless, Ægir. You are dangerous. You are a danger to this city. At the same time, this city is also a danger to you.
Hm?
Look at them. They are... eating scraps of the Sea Terrors' remains. They don't fear these monsters... No, they don't even know what monsters are.
They feed when they are hungry. As a monster does. And humans... humans know what fear is.
So... Are they still human?
All I know is that they're trying their best to live.
From that perspective, there's nothing that sets humans and monsters apart.
When they live like that... can you really say they are alive?
The High Inquisitor told me to see it for myself, and only now do I understand what he meant.
In this city, the biggest problem isn't whatever its citizens are doing. It's that they are still alive in this manner—This is the most abnormal thing of all.
Like you, they are dangerous, and this danger must be kept under control, just like how mistakes must be corrected.
You haven't given up.
Now I really can't let you go.
I already told you. I still have something to take care of, and I am in a hurry.
Looks like we will have to do this again, then.
Ægir, I know you are hurt, and you must be exhausted after fighting the Sea Terrors.
But I will not go easy on you.
What's this? A small shell?
It wasn't you, Ægir... You wouldn't throw this at me.
Perhaps one of the locals vomiting their guts out on the floor? No, it can't be them. They don't even know what it is they are stuffing into their mouths. They wouldn't move against me.
Is it... from the window over to the side?
Who is it?!
Who threw this? Step forward!
Hahh...
You? Did you throw this at me? Are you still trying to get in my way, to protect this Ægir?
Uh, yes! It's me!
Anita, stop it with your nonsense.
Grandma Petra?! Y-You should stay inside...
I did it, Your Honor.
Did you just call me 'Your Honor?'
I know who exactly you are. These ones know nothing.
There's someone still sane here...?
Old woman, perhaps your eyes aren't as good as they used to be. Please step back. We are taking care of something dangerous here.
Look, Your Honor. My eyes are as perfect as they can be. Even if the sky's pitch black, I will have no trouble hitting you as long as you forget to dodge.
Oh, tiny seashells, beautiful waves♪
One shell after another, one beating wave after another...
You admitted it yourself. Now I must—
Inquisitor... Your Honor! Grandma Petra's sick... Please don't blame her?
She's sick? No wonder. Yes, I can see that.
Oh, Little Anita. Enough nonsense. How am I sick?
Grandma Petra, please stop. Please.
Sick... sick!
What's sick is Sal Viento itself! Yes—This place, and all its people! And you, almighty Inquisitor... You are sick as well!
You, take your senile grandmother home.
I have more important things to deal with.
What important things? What's more important than being alive?
As an Inquisitor, there are things that I have to safeguard even with my life. For example, all the things that the law says are correct... as well as our country itself.
You will use your life... to safeguard whose life?

I... Whatever... I shouldn't waste any more of my time with you. Your old age and your illness have done a number on your faculties, citizen. I can overlook your crazy behavior so long as you stand aside and let me take care of what's important.
Your Honor, I'm afraid I don't understand this right or wrong, this country of yours.
I just want to ask you this. This thing you are trying to uphold. We were never part of it, isn't that right?
What...?!
You Inquisitors took our people away. You said they were disrupting order, that they assaulted someone while they were stealing food. They just wanted to live.
That wasn't me... but if they committed a crime, then that's how it should be.
And they've never been back.
You brought your bad guys away, but did that improve our lives?
When you remove all the factors that bring chaos to society in time, there will naturally be order.
Yet there is less and less food to go by... Those of us who got on in years either die of hunger or disease. Even some of our young couldn't endure.

You speak of order... but all you want is for us to die quietly, isn't it? That way, there will be no more noise, and you upper class folks can finally lie down on your fluffy beds for a quiet night of sleep.
That's not it.
All of Iberia is suffering. Not just you.
Shouldn't a criminal who steals resources and harms the people be stopped? Suppose there are ten meals before us. They should feed ten Iberians.
If no one is there to tell them how much they should get, they will fight to no end. In the end, only one of the ten will remain.
The Inquisition draws the boundary of right and wrong amid this chaos and guides the masses to the right path. Otherwise the whole country would've long become a heap of sand.
Everything you said... I don't get it, and it's nothing I can see.
We don't have a way out. When we truly wanted one, where were you?
I... I want to help you. I am working hard to become a full-fledged Inquisitor. I took this path because I want to help the people, and I trust that this is the only way.
Help us...?
I hoped, imagined, thirsted, even dreamt of someone coming to save us. I screamed through so many nights till my throat was hoarse.
You never came.
Will you Inquisitors only come when someone has done something wrong?
Look at these old bones. I never imagined I'd live this long. What a tragedy. Every day is filled with nothing but pain.
Look, I'm attacking you, Your Honor.
Take me away! Take me, and end me! Put me out of my misery!
Hurry, do it! I can't wait no longer—I've waited all these years! *Cough* *cough*...
I don't want to fight you. Your anger... is understandable. My sword should not...
Don't force my hand, citizen.
*Cough* *Cough*...
Grandma!
Oh... Anita... You came to the plaza too.
It's dark... Why is there no singing yet?
Singer, you said you are a singer. Why aren't you singing? Where is your harp? I want to dance. I need a tune.
Look, Your Honor. You're more useless than a wandering singer. She sings for us. She's even brave enough... to stand in front of the rest of us.
I'm not scared of monsters, and I'm not scared of death. What I am scared of is this life. Am I crazy, Anita?
Grandma, it's okay. You're shaking. It's cold here. Why don't we go back? Lie down and get some rest. You'll feel better.
Okay... Okay. Let's go. We'll go home. It isn't like I know where else to go anyway.
You...!
Did you change your mind? If you changed your mind, you can still take me away. That, or kill me. At the very least... I would get to die like a person.
Do it while I'm still alive, Your Honor. I don't have much time left.
Come, let's go... Singer, come with us. I want to hear you sing.
I will let you go... for now.
But I will be back!
And you, Ægir! I'm watching you, and I will get to the end of this! Don't think you can get out of this one!
Phew... That was close.
Singer, are you okay? You look white as a sheet.
I'm fine.
You don't need to act so tough anymore. The Inquisitor is gone.
I'm not afraid of her.
I know. I can tell you aren't afraid. What would someone like you be afraid of?
She's not a bad person.
And it would be a good thing for you if I leave this place.
A good thing? There's nothing good here.
Grandma might've said all that, but she doesn't really hate the Inquisitor. She hates something else. I know it.
Now dance. Haha, I love your singing...
Stop spinning around, Grandma. You're tired. The singer will sing for you. Isn't that right?
Give me your hand, singer. Let me take a look at your wound.
No...
I can't come near you because it's dangerous, right?
It's okay. I trust you. If anything happens, you will win.
Look, your wounds aren't so bad at all. No more bleeding.
Singer, you are amazing. You heal up so quickly.
It could've been quicker.
But even so, you don't look well to me, and the house you picked was blown to pieces. I doubt even you can get a good sleep there now. Why don't you come home with me and Grandma Petra?
That's right. Stay with us. Don't let them come look for any more trouble with you.
No...
Okay, enough 'No's, 'No need's, and 'I have other things to do.' I am sick of them.
I can still...
Ugh...
Look at you. Out of strength, no? You need someone to look after you, and you definitely need some sleep.
I also think the person you're looking for probably doesn't want to see you pushing yourself like this.
What was that... just now?
None of my business anymore...
I shouldn't ask questions. I have my answer now. I know the way. I know where to go.
I need to go straight... Straight ahead.
Tomorrow, everyone will have food to eat. I... No, we... All of us will get to live.
'We want to survive, so we must love one another even more.'
'For love is the inseparable bond between us.'
'I will become stronger.'
'Our people will likewise become stronger.'

lines.st: The man limps forward toward the pitch-black seas until he comes upon an object blocking his way.

lines.x: This...
Her harp. It's here.
(Tries to play the harp like Skadi does)
(Chords)
Sounds horrible.
This is useless. I will leave it here to rot.
There is no end... The fog is everywhere...

lines.st: A glowing mist rises from the bottomless abyss and engulfs her whole.
That instant, all her senses are cut off from the rest of the world.
Perhaps she can feel there is something right in front of her.
Before her is the entire ocean.
She puts her whole strength into striking the enormous yet almost shapeless object before her.
Everything she can feel immediately crumbles away.
An ice cold yet burning hot liquid swallows her. These are the ocean's currents, as well as its blood.
The very last dance has concluded. The seas have calmed, and she can no longer hear her people's singing.
She saved... No, she has no idea what it is that she actually saved.
Likewise, she does not have the faintest idea what it is she triumphed over.

She paddles her arms and kicks her feet as though her life depends on it, yet the waters of the ocean pay no heed to her willpower. Like solid blocks of iron, the water has become cold and stiff, squeezing her body from all directions.
She stops moving, leaving herself to the mercy of the sea.
As she sinks further and further into the ocean, a black shadow envelops her.
The countless voices begin to materialize around her. Some of them are crying and some... are murmuring.
The sea is talking to her.
Its cold, lightless form has been restored, as have her eyes. She cannot see the path upward. She is sinking downward further and further, just as she is rising upward more and more.
At a place deeper than even the sea's deepest abysses, she is lifted up by the warm waters, as though the waters themselves are the waiting arms of her family.
She opens her lips. The saltwater gushes into her throat and dissolves her completely before reconstructing her anew.
She has found her singing voice once more.

lines.x: Uhh...
Singer, you're awake! Good morning!
Was someone just singing?
I-Is this a song? I don't know how to sing, but you were humming this while you were asleep.
Ow! Singer... You are grabbing too hard...
You...

lines.st: The girl's figure turns faint. Just like in the sea.
No, the one in the sea was someone else.
The dream is ripping her nerves to shreds.
The musical notes are dancing, twisting themselves into the cries of her family.

lines.x: Mother...

lines.st: They are dying before her eyes, and there is nothing she can do to prevent it.

lines.x: Monsters...
Monsters.
Monsters!

lines.st: They are screaming, as is everyone around her.
She must grab her weapon.

lines.x: Ah!
*cough*... Singer, your box... It's really hurting my chest when you press it against me...

lines.st: Her blade pierces through a monster in the midst of feeding on a person.
The monster turns around.
She sees a face she often sees in the mirror.
The box falls on the ground, bouncing slightly before it comes to a stop. The object stored therein tumbles inside the box a little but does not fall out.

lines.x: A-Are you letting go? That really scared me...
Singer, I thought you were going to kill me.
If I were going to kill you, I could've done that easily.
But you don't want to kill me.
You can tell?
I can tell.
But I don't know.
Eh?
R-Running out again...? Singer, where are you going?!
Sir... The tides are rising again.
The colors of the sea... That patch of color has changed.
What is there in the water? I-Is it shells and finfish? Why would they suddenly show up in the water? And why are they growing closer and closer to the shore?
From far deeper places, they made their way here by following currents that normally would not exist.
What?!
I thought... the reason they headed into the sea, claiming it would get them food, was some absurd, unfounded belief. I thought, in their despair, they were fooled into doing this...
But I never imagined... they are actually dealing with the sea? They are using their own men as fodder to feed the monsters of the sea, and the monsters then also feed them in return?
This mutualism is horrifying... and wretched...! No wonder there are so many Sea Terrors here!
But I thought I put an end to it yesterday...!
Not all corrections are fruitful. This is something you must understand.
Am I in the wrong, High Inquisitor? I just wanted to save them.
To right a wrong, you either bring what strayed from righteousness back onto the right path—
But... What do the people here even see as right?
You can't grow any food in the lands and seas of Sal Viento anymore.
This was a foregone conclusion decades ago, and to fight it would be futile. The nomadic city, the society, and the people who live in it... They are all slowly marching towards their death.
They asked me... If... If there isn't a way out... How are they supposed to see right from wrong?
—Or perhaps make that which is wrong no more.
Sir...! You don't mean...
You are doubting me.
I... No, of course not. I just...
They may have turned out like this... but they were once Iberian citizens. I want to protect them, and I hope I can help these inhabitants... so that they can make our country a better place.
You should have doubts.
Huh?
In making a judgment, rely on no one but yourself!
Yes, sir...
Look out at the sea. What do you see?
I see... a threat.
Don't regurgitate your memorized answers.
Yes, sir!
The sea... is flowing. It's ever changing.
Remember everything that you see.
Change has happened, is happening, and will happen. If you can't see it clearly for yourself, you will find yourself lost.
Sir, what is that under your feet?!
What do you think?
A Sea Terror... Did we miss one when they attacked last night?! No, doesn't seem like it... This one wandered off the beach... It looks like it's climbed a long way... It's not quite the same...
I... uh... I should know this...
This is an evil that must be eradicated.
I get it... I can see it... It's the whole truth of this deal of theirs.
So this is what becomes of anyone who wanders into the sea... This is not just the monsters' nest. It's their breeding ground.
Uhh...
Doesn't that mean that all the people here will become monsters?!
(Grips scabbard)
High Inquisitor, I need to head back to the city.

lines.st: Skadi sits on the shore.
Carried by the winds, the sea waves beat the shore ceaselessly, but never reaching her dress.

lines.x: *pant*... *pant*... Phew... S-Singer, you're too fast...
But not fast enough.
Huh?
You managed to follow me again.
H-Haha... Singer, this is my home... I know this place much better than you.
I should leave.
Leave? What do you mean?
I mean leave this place.
Huh? Did you figure out where to go to find your friend?
At the very least... I should keep my distance from you.
Huh? Why's that?
The Inquisitor was right about one thing.
She talked about a lot of things...
I am a danger to all of you.
Because of what happened last night? You saved us, Singer. We are very thankful of what you did for us.
I saved you? Is that what you think?
If I'd missed even one of the Sea Terrors, it could've torn you to pieces as it marched on.
You would've been killed.
I brought it here. Therefore, I killed you.
No, that's not how it is...
How is it not? This has happened before.
They died because of me. They despised me, they were scared of me, and they blamed me for it.
They... They were wrong!
You didn't mean to do that.
I didn't mean to?
I don't know.
Uhh...
After all that, I'm not sure... anymore.

lines.st: She thinks back to her dream.
Ever since that final battle, she has had that dream countless of times.
The Abyssal Hunters never stay in one place. They always march forward.
Yet Skadi was trapped there.
She has no idea what is happening to her.

lines.x: I thought that if I killed it, I would find the answer.

lines.st: Killing that gigantic monster.
Things would get better for everyone. No one would cry anymore, and no one would scream anymore.
She just had to kill it.
And she killed it...
Yet an even greater problem engulfed her.
The Abyssal Hunters never ask why. They simply do.
But Skadi wants to know why.
She has no idea what is happening to her.

lines.x: Singer...? You don't look so good.
You are hurt, and you spent so much of your energy. You haven't completely recovered yet, have you?
You should come back with me and get some rest. You will feel much better once you get more sleep.
Why aren't you afraid of me?
Hm?
Your grandmother called me a monster, while your friends all looked at me with animosity.
These kinds of gazes are everywhere... I got used to them long ago.
But what about you? When you look at me, what do you see?
You... You are a singer.
A singer. That's what I told you, and you believed it. That Inquisitor, on the other hand, did not. If I tell you I'm a monster now, will you believe me?
I won't...
Why are you always following me?
Huh?
Anything that I can give you, you are free to take, yet you don't want them.
You don't want to kill me either.
K-Kill you? No, Singer, why would I want to kill you?
When I do something for you, you pay me something in return. It's always either that or the other way around.
Whenever you exchange something, there exists a promise. It's only when promises exist that there's trust.
Promise?
You don't know what that is.
(Shakes head)
Listen up. You helped me, so I will give you what you want. This is what a promise is.
I don't want anything...
You are lying.
I can smell as much with my nose as my eyes can see.
Uhh...
You told me this is your home, and that's how you know how to find me so quickly. That's a lie too.
If you don't tell me the truth, I can't trust you.
No, Singer, don't go!
It's not that I'm not telling the truth. I just... don't understand.
Singer, to all of us... To me, you are still a mystery. You came from the outside, and I've never met anyone like you...
You want to know how I guessed that you are here. It's not that I'm trying to lie. I'm just... embarrassed.
This is... the outskirts. It's as far as it gets from the city.
I know that you are hiding from us, and I thought this is where you would probably come too if you wanted to run away.
'Too?'
I come here often myself. Of course, I don't tell Sheetmetal or anyone. They aren't happy when they find me running this far away.
Look at that chunk over there. It's tall. I climb on there sometimes. The stronger the wind gets, the farther I can see.
You are curious about what's outside.
I don't know. I... I sometimes wonder about how it's like outside. It's pretty pointless, I know, and they kept telling me that too, so all I do is imagine it all by myself here.
This is the furthest I've gone whenever I go outside to scavenge. Outside... There's nothing useful outside.
If you're curious about what's outside, you just have to walk a little further.
I used to go to the west side. Sheetmetal would warn me that there were monsters outside, and we would die if we ever stepped out.
Singer, you are from the outside. I am following you around because I have lots and lots of questions... I want to know if what Sheetmetal said is actually true.
Perhaps.
If you head outside, you will die.
Phew... so it's true...
You aren't disappointed.
Singer, if I have the chance, I think I want to see it for myself.
It doesn't matter if there are monsters there... I don't think I'm scared of them.
Hm?
The Inquisitor said I'm not afraid of monsters because I don't know what they would do to me.
I think she's right about that.
She made that up.
*cough*, *cough*...
I'm not afraid of them either.
Haha... That's gotta be because you know the monsters can't do anything to you.
Not like me. Last night was the first time I got a good look of those monsters. I didn't know what was going to happen to me. My heart was racing like never before.
Then, when I woke up this morning, the weather felt so much better than I've ever experienced. I could feel the wind, and I could see the sea waves splashing ever closer.
This day has been different than all the other days I've lived, because I don't know what's going to happen.
Sounds like you're excited.
Excited?
Fear and excitement are pretty much the same. Both get your heart racing and your blood flowing.
Am I sick?
You are not sick. To some people out there, this is what it means to be alive.
I didn't know it feels this good to be alive. Is it like this outside every day?
It must feel better than here.
Can I really go outside...?
My box... Once my box is full.
I need to... get more food. I need to make sure Grandma Petra's all set.
I... Argh!

lines.st: Excited, the girl loses her footing.
Just as she is about to fall into the sea, a hand reaches out and grabs her.

lines.x: The monsters will eat you if you're so careless.
Singer... you saved me again.
And you could've saved me the trouble if you didn't come.
Haha... S-Sorry...
Huh? Huh?
My leg... Something's grabbing my leg.
It's slippery... and heavy!
There they are again... Everywhere, too. What a pain.
Ugh... Singer, I-I'm losing my grip...
Close your eyes.
Hm?
And then let go.
Can I really...?
You can either believe me or not. The choice is yours.

lines.st: The girl closes her eyes and lets go as she is told.

The force gripping onto her leg quickly disappears. She can hear all sorts of tiny trinkets splashing into the water. Just before she herself touches the surface of the sea, a force pulls her upward and sends her back to dry land again.
—She lands face first.

lines.x: *cough* *cough* *cough*.
It's not easy to control the strength and angle while I'm dealing with a person.
I-It's okay. I'm fine.
(Smells herself)
My bleeding's stopped.
Why are they still so excited? Have there always been so many Sea Terrors here?
Are these the same monsters from last night?
More or less.
I've never seen them before.
You should stay away from this place for a couple of days. Now go back.
Singer.
Stop tugging my skirt... You aren't a child.
I just want to say... I think I know what it is that I want now. I want to hear you sing.
Oh...
And that's because... I don't know what songs are like. I heard you humming in your sleep, but I didn't understand it.
Alright.
Singer, will you do it? Is this what it means to make a promise?
More or less.
(Frowns)
My harp.
Huh? Oh yeah, singer, where did your harp go?
I'll go look for it...
Okay. I'll help.
So does this mean you'll come back with me? G-Great!
Is this something to be so happy about?
Hey, Drywall, good morning!
Sheetmetal...
Oh yeah, where did Sheetmetal go? You never go anywhere without each other.
Sheetmetal.
Are you looking out... to the sea?
Does this mean... It was Sheetmetal who got the red seashell last night?
*Sigh*...
Woodframe, my condition got worse.
My chest is bloating like it's about to blow.
I don't even want to eat. Leave me alone.
*Sigh*...
Singer, welcome to our home sweet home again!
Ugh... agh...
Your grandmother...
Grandma Petra is still sleeping. She's been lying down ever since we got back last night.
She's probably tired, getting so worked up like that. I didn't expect her to run out and talk to the Inquisitor like that. She was different.
You're right. That was very amazing. She fought off the Inquisitor.
Hmn... ha...
She fought off.. the Inquisitor?! Singer, if the Inquisitor catches you saying that, she'll be really mad.
I don't care if she does.
But if she wants another go...
She won't come to you looking for trouble again, right?
It doesn't matter to me whose trouble she's looking for.
(Strokes case)
Oh, yeah, why don't we go look for your harp?
Hold on.
Eh?
You got stuff on your head.
Whoa! All this green, slimy stuff... W-What is this?
That's seaweed.
Did it get on my head when I tripped?
Probably.
Is this stuff... useful in any way? Looks kinda like seagrass. Can I use it as bedding once it's dried? Grandma Petra's back is hurting her, so this will come in handy.
Give it to me.
Take some seashells and use them to grind up these seaweeds. Don't wash off the slime.
Add enough seawater to completely cover the seaweeds. Put it all in a jar and seal it up, then wait half a year.
You can drink it once the liquid's greenish color has faded. That's when it's fermented.
Singer...?
What's wrong? Is there something you didn't understand?
No... I just don't think you've ever talked to me this much in one go.
Yeah.
Don't throw away your seashells either. Wash them and lay them out to dry. You can use them to drink alcohol and such.
Alcohol? What's that?
It's written right there. This place used to be a bar, a place for alcohol.
So this word means alcohol!
Alcohol can supposedly bring happiness and help us forget all our worries.
Does it work?
Not at all...
It does taste pretty good, though.
Did you drink this stuff often back home...? Back in Ægir?
Not exactly.
I would drink something similar before dances.
It must have been very fun. Oh, I get it now! Alcohol doesn't bring happiness, but we drink alcohol when we're happy!
Does everyone outside, uh, drink alcohol? Is everyone out there always so happy?
Not necessarily.
I get it. They definitely aren't as happy as I am now. Singer, thanks for teaching me how to make alcohol. I really wanna drink it with you right now.
Your hand movement is wrong.
If you grind it like that, it's not going to grind up very well. Try a different angle. Use the sharpest point and follow the seaweed's pattern.
Control your rhythm and your breathing... Make your hand and your whole body one with the sea breeze that's brushing against your skin.
Like that—One stroke, and it's shredded.
You are staring at me. Do you still want another go? Now that there's only one of you, it'll be over very quickly.
No... I don't think I will.
This is yours.
Hmm?
This is my harp.
Sheetmetal found it.
He saw you drop it outside last night.
Oh, that's great! Singer, now we don't need to go look for your harp!
He brought it back to our house... and... and he told me to give it to you.
That's all he said before he left...
Thanks.
Huh? Oh...
(Plucks strings)
Huh...? This melody... You were humming it while you were asleep last night.
It's always in my head.
Is this a song that you and your partner used to sing?
We have a lot of songs like this back home. We sing... as a way to talk to each other.
When I left, the singing was gone too. I'm all by myself, and this song is all that I have left.
No wonder this song sounds so sad.
(Plucks strings)
Singer, remember our promise?! You got your harp back. Does that mean you can sing for us now?
We're all waiting. Right, Grandma Petra?
Mmn... Uhh...
Grandma probably can't talk today. She would sing along with you if she could.
Ah... Ahhh...
Singer, Grandma wants to hear your song.
Alright.

lines.st: Clad in her red dress, the wandering singer sits in the middle of the group. She closes her eyes as her fingers stroke the strings of her harp.

The notes and the lyrics flow out from her naturally as she plunks away at her harp strings, permeating every corner of the rundown bar. The melody twists and expands in the air as though this is where it belongs.

lines.x: A hunter is walking on seacoast way♪
With homeland left a mile behind, his path lies ahead♪
From his forebears and children, he was torn♪
Into the seas his lover was swept away, floating in the deep, dead♪
I noticed a change in your expression. Did you feel something, perhaps?
Nothing.

Not a surprise. Your reflexes are still rather slow. Look at the water before you. The fluctuation of the level, the frequency of the waves, and the movement of its luster. All of these are information.
It'll be high tide soon. We should go.
A hunter is walking on seacoast way♪
With homeland left a mile behind, they must lament and pray♪
There is no end to his path♪
The road ahead is shrouded in gray♪
She's singing.
How could she sing in front of these people in this rundown house?
And... they are listening to her.
The ones who hang their heads are still looking straight at the floor. The ones who don't speak are still silent as ever. Her singing has no way to reach their cold, hard husks, but their eyes...
Do they understand it somehow?

lines.st: A hunter is walking on seacoast way♪
The singer stops her fingers, but the harmony formed by her harp and voice together does not simply disappear because of that.
It yet remains with the wanderer herself, creating an open seam in the thick dust.
A few sprouts land in that crevice just as others begin to sprout from it.

lines.x: Grandma Petra?
What's wrong?
She... fell asleep.
She never sleeps so peacefully. Most of the time, she starts screaming the moment she closes her eyes, grasping at nothing.
I thought she'd want to dance when she heard you sing. I mean, she's always chirping about how she wants to dance.
Drywall?
I'm going to die. There's seawater in my eyes... It's salty. Not tasty at all.
Whoa... Are you... crying?
Crying? What is that?
It's a kind of emotion that flows out from the deepest depths of your body...
It is...? I don't think I understand.
Singer, I like your songs. You don't like to talk, but you have so much to tell when you are singing.
Could you sing it again? I want to hear you talk about lots more.
(Plucks strings)

lines.st: The sea breeze envelops the singer's fingers and wafts into her nostrils. The smell here has changed.
It's now high tide.

lines.x: Food...
We'll have food again...
To the beach.
Priest!

lines.st: The masses rush outside.
The melody of her song has stopped.
The beach is lined with food.
The wishes of the masses have been answered by the sea.

lines.x: Fins.
Shells...
Edible.
Yes, it's edible.
More... more... more...
I spilled some.
Pick it up. Swallow it. More...
Take them. Take them back.
Take them back. More and more.
Priest...
Priest.

lines.st: They stop for a moment and look up at their brother.

lines.x: My brothers and sisters, enjoy your breakfast.
This is a reward for our good deeds. Because we prevailed against our hunger and illnesses, we become more faithful, more united, and more harmonious with one another.
I'm surprised you don't ever get tired of putting on that show when you do it at practically every city you visit.
Show? Hardly. It is a joy to share His greatness and benevolence with the unenlightened across the lands.
They don't understand a word you just said.
All of us are one and the same to the vast ocean.

lines.st: The bishop lovingly taps the arm of a man who is stuffing his mouth full with food.

lines.x: Come, dear brother. Share these fins with your suffering brethren. You must understand. This small piece will grant him satisfaction, which will in turn enrich your soul.
As for you, you must thank your brother. Thank him for giving you what could have been his.
When the two of you stand together, hand in hand, shoulder to shoulder, you share your life with each other, and that makes you ever more powerful.
Thank you... Father... sir.
Very good, dear child.
The promise isn't between you and it. It's between them and it with you as their intermediator.
Promise? No, a promise is mutual. These suffering souls are far from able to satisfy the needs of the sea.
The sea grants them their desires, and I help them so that their voices are heard.
As you already count yourself a part of us, you must know the rules.
Please forgive my negligence. There are certainly more suitable turns of phrase. You are using their flesh and blood to lay down the path to what it is you want within the chapel.
Whichever the case, you don't need to show up here personally every time.
How do you know what I want?
It's hard not to become addicted to the pleasure of superiority.
Nonsense.
As the sea's most faithful creation, there is nary an ounce of falsehood to my position.
I stand here because I wish to save them from the bottom of my heart. Without me, they would long have become a pile of rotten meat strewn across these lands.
Disease and starvation. As long as we can fight against these tangible enemies, they are not our most difficult hardships. Our most difficult hardships come from the deepest parts of our souls.
When we find ourselves unable to fight these disasters, with destruction unavoidable, that is when we are faced with true hardship.
How many towns like this are still standing in Iberia?
This country's vast territory has long been unable to fulfill its people's needs. Nonetheless, they remain in their cramped flesh, either isolating themselves from one another or brawling.
Well, it's admirable that you care so much about Iberia.
You are wrong again. It is the suffering of the people that I care about, not the borders arbitrarily drawn on these lands.
Isn't this in the nature of us all?
Bound by the limitations of our flesh and blood, it's difficult for us to break away from our obsession with ourselves by our own means, and so we choose to fight, not to understand.
If this goes on, even the strongest of individuals cannot escape defeat. Hardships engulf every corner. You have seen it yourself and wavered at the sight of that misery.
Is this necessary?
Perhaps I'm not telling you enough.
The truth is always worth telling, whether the listener is as foolish as you or not.
Perhaps we should treat this conversation as nothing more than a way to pass the time, seeing as we are working together.
Well, your way with words is on another level. It's no wonder so many have found themselves enthralled by your words.
You do not yet understand. I am most sincere. I ask nothing from them. I do not believe in the power of threats either. I simply wish to point them toward a new possibility.
Their bodies' weakness will forever limit their cognition. You think I am ensnaring their souls with words, but the truth is far from it.

I am helping their hearts to hear the most basic needs of their bodies. The teachings of sages can bring nothing but brief, blind faith. The strength of the body is what grants them the right to see more.
There are creatures in the sea. They have no eyes, their bodies are miniscule, and they have no means of mobility. They can only drift with the tides.
Is the ocean that they see so different than what we see? How do creatures with no eyes grasp the holiness and profoundness of the sea?
You and your colleagues never understood how and where your vile yet sturdy bodies came to be, nor can you see how the weak struggle.
Who, then, is the truly arrogant?

Is it me, despite the fact I am helping them, or is it those believers of false gods, who, as the perils grow ever greater, use their words to turn true despair into false hope, to deceive and to control the pitiable weak?
Or perhaps—It's you and your foolish cohorts, who remain ever unwilling to face the vast sea bottoms?

I am sharing with them what my eyes can see so that they may broaden their horizons and know they still have true hope. They may choose for themselves whether they will rot with the land or be reborn as one with the sea.
Not only can this possibility save them from starvation and disease, it is also the sea's generosity for the land that insists on consuming.
This is not a conquest. It's understanding. It's acceptance.
I am pointing them in a new direction and giving them a better life. In the end, they will walk toward the truth, just as I once did.
This is my life's work. Your false flesh has confined your soul and left you short-sighted. You cannot see true greatness. You shall be forgiven.
Hmph. Sounds like they are not the only ones obsessed.
Phew, Singer, we almost missed out on this.
There's so much food... Everyone's hungry, and we finally have something to eat.
1, 2, 3... I'll bring these back and grind them up. I can make soup for Grandma Petra with them. We'll have this for dinner tonight. Sound good, Bench?
Wgh... aogh...
No, don't eat them like that!
The shells are too hard. They'll break your teeth! We have to boil them until they're soft before we can eat them.
Singer, why don't you take some too?
S-Singer? What are you standing there for?

lines.st: Skadi simply stares at the man named Drywall. He is on his knees, eating all that he can see on the beach.
His eyes are red. His tears have already dried up.
He is starving, and so is everyone here.
Skadi lets go of the girl.
The girl's eyes are sparkling. She cannot wait to join the crowd of feeding people.

lines.x: Slow down, brother. This is all yours. Look around you. All this will be yours in the end.
Now, open your heart, accept yourself, embrace others, learn to be trusting, and learn to sacrifice.
The fighting has long come to an end, and the hardships will cease. Let us stand together. Our souls, flesh, and belief will grow strong, just like this eternal ocean under our feet.
Please allow me to keep my distance.
Oh?
I've been having certain... digestion issues. I'm afraid I might not be able to hold it. I'll throw up on your face.
—Brother, you dropped your seashell. Let your brother hold onto it...
Interesting. You must be in a good mood today, given your choice of words.
Whatever you say.
The message from the sea has you excited. You can wait for our celebration no longer.
Then go. Find her. Find your old friend, and share this joy with her.
Found you...

lines.st: Skadi quickly makes it through the crowd. Her eyes are fixated on a single person.
That person appears as though she is waiting for her as well.

lines.x: What are you doing?
Shh.
Come. Look at him.
Do you smell it?

lines.st: Skadi takes out a seashell.
With the seashell in her hand, she dashes toward Gladiia.
Gladiia remains calm and unflinching. She seems to have expected this.
The seashell stabs her hand, leaving no marks at all.

lines.x: Your stance isn't elegant enough. Let me show you.

lines.st: Gladiia presses the seashell against Skadi's chest, pushing her up against the wall.

lines.x: How many times have we danced together, Skadi? Twice, maybe thrice?
You are a good dance partner.
Where is she?
You care about her.
Should I not?
She is rather safe for the moment.
Proof?
I didn't know you were in the market for something so pointless.
This is making me think your time on land has ruined your nose, Hunter.
Your whole body reeks.
Trust me. I want to vomit far more than you do right now.
She's with you alright.
Looks like your nose hasn't gone completely bad.

Fortunately, it seems you haven't quite fallen to the level of those land-born cubs just yet. At the very least, you haven't come crying to me, asking me why I would take her away and what I am doing here.
Asking questions has never been a way of solving things. Not for us.
'Us?'
I don't even know if you are still one of us.
Looks like you have a lot of questions on your mind.
(Strokes case)
If you aren't sure, you might want to think twice about picking a fight.
Otherwise, you'd just be giving your enemy an opportunity.
You are not my target.
Then what did you come all this way for?
Don't you already know?
You've been looking for her, but will your doubts really go away when you finally find her?
Her memory's shot. She can't tell me much.
Take a look at yourself. You are barely better than being locked in a cage.
You may not be working for me anymore, but let me teach you one more thing.
Remember this. A hunter chases her prey because she must hunt it down. Chasing blindly will bring you only emotional comfort.
Are you so weak now that you are chasing after these phantom illusions, Skadi?
How long has it been since you danced? You've walked too much. Your skin is very dry. Have you forgotten the proper rhythm for your breathing?
Let me ask you again. What are you here for?

lines.st: Skadi looks at the bishop.
The bishop stands only a short distance away. He looks at Skadi as well, his gaze piercing through the crowd.
Skadi tightens her grip on her box.
A courteous and gentle smile appears on the bishop's face. He points in a direction and gestures invitingly.

lines.x: Not going to make your move?
I thought you two were together?
I don't really care.
Doesn't seem like I can say that about you, though. You are the one who needs that weight off your shoulders, not me.

lines.st: One by one, the inhabitants walk up to the bishop with food still in their arms. They greet the bishop as though he is truly their brother and friend.
—Just like a normal afternoon in your average Iberian town.
Skadi slowly lets go.

lines.x: Stop with the mysteries! Who is he?!
I could tell you, but would you believe me?

lines.st: Skadi looks at the chapel on the top of the hill.
Beneath it, the bishop is waving goodbye to the inhabitants.

lines.x: Uh... Ughh...
Slowly. Careful not to trip.
Did you hear that? The sound of the waves. Thump, thump, thump, just like our heartbeats.
The food you eat and the water you drink are all gifts from the sea. My brothers and sisters, do you feel the energy in you surging throughout your body and on to your heart?
Embrace the vastness before you! This is your hope for rebirth!

lines.st: Skadi looks at the people onshore.
The crowd has started to disperse. The girl has picked a basket full of seafood. At last, she looks up from the ground and begins to search for someone on the empty shore.

lines.x: Huh? Where's the singer?
I took my eyes off her for just a moment, and she disappeared again.
*Sigh*.
Oh well, she knows the way back. She's probably looking for her friend again.
Let's go, Bench. Let's bring the food back.
Uhh...
Bench, I want to sing. Let's sing together.
Do you remember the song that the singer sang? It's okay if you don't. I mean, you don't know how to talk. How about I sing it for you?
With homeland left a mile behind♪
Yea...
His path lies ahead♪
Ahh...
Why are you stopping me? *Sigh*. You think it's terrible, don't you? I think so, too.
Of course I can't sing as well as the singer!
But if I keep at it, I will get better, right?
Hehe... His path lies ahead...
I need to go now. If I don't get going, he will be suspicious.
Captain! What are you planning?
Is it a teammate you're looking for? Why didn't you say so?
Or is it an enemy you want? If you were my enemy, you would've killed me when I tried to kill you with the seashell just now.

lines.st: Gladiia shakes her head.

lines.x: You need to understand what it is that you want first. A hesitant hunter can't spot traps and will only find herself the prey of others. Is that what you want?
Singer, your eyes seem a little different.
Hm?
When you looked at us before, it felt no different from when you looked at the monsters from the sea.
But now you are willing to teach me how to make seaweed alcohol, and you sing for us.
Of course you are different from the monsters.
Monsters wouldn't keep pestering me to sing for them. Monsters can't even speak. Most of the time, they are dirty and dumb, and they know nothing other than to eat.
S-So... will those monsters from the sea eat us?
If they are hungry, they will.
So they come ashore for the same reason we go out to the sea. They are looking for things to eat.
Are you sympathizing with them?
Going hungry is tough.
I don't think that's how the ones who get eaten see it.
Right... I don't want to be eaten by the monsters like this either.
I've never thought about it before, but now... Uh, I want to listen to your songs just a little longer. I want to teach Bench how to sing too, just like how Grandma taught me so many things.
In that case, you need to live as long as your grandmother.
Yep! At the very least, I'm not worried about anything right now.
And that's because... Singer, you are still here. You will help us, and fight off these monsters, like, bam bam bam!

lines.st: Gladiia has left.
Skadi looks out at the sea.

Unlike the dead silence of the day before, its waters are filled with living creatures. They cling onto each other and pile on top of one another. The creature at the very top stretches out its limb. It camouflages itself as a part of the sea waves, while striking the shore's reefs with each beating wave.
Atop the hill, the chapel doors are wide open.
The people ashore have already gone home.
The monsters of the sea, eyeless as they may be, merely 'stare' at the sight.

lines.x: My heartbeat... is getting faster.

lines.st: It isn't just her heartbeat. She looks at her hands and sees something that only the eyes of a hunter can see. Unable to withstand the impact of her blood flow, her skin is trembling at a high speed.
The off-key singing reverberates in her eardrums, all while the odor of the sea irritates her nostrils.

lines.x: I see... Right.
I am still a hunter...

lines.st: A hunter chases her prey, and a hunter hunts her prey, but Abyssal Hunters dance atop the fangs and claws of their fallen prey. A hunter who has lost her partner is naturally lonely and despondent.
She is a hunter herself, and not the kind after bounties. Running away any further would be meaningless. What she must do now is to turn around and join the hunt.
They are the ones who should be afraid.
It is only now that Skadi has finally forgiven herself.
She dashes toward the Sea Terror-filled tides.
There is a stench. A stench lingers in the air of the chapel.

lines.x: That should take care of it, I think? We should have enough for me, Grandma, and Bench...
Oh, and the singer too. She will come back, right? We can't let her go hungry.
We still have... some. We can put the rest in the box.
Uh, Uncle Fireplace, you wanted some? O-Okay, I can give you some first.
Whoa, I think that's seaweed on your arm! Let me have it! I'll make the... uh... seaweed alcohol that the singer taught me to make!
Huh? Where did Bench go? I only took my eyes off for a moment.
(Makes children noises)

lines.st: A red-clad figure appears before him.

lines.x: Ah... Songs... Ahhh...
Stop grabbing...
This is yours.
Whaa...
Stop chewing it... It's not something you can eat.
Woodframe... Woodframe...
Don't call her...
Yeah, give her my harp, will you?
Tell her... No, I guess you can't talk.
This isn't a goodbye.
I'll go ask a question. Then, I'll tell her myself.

lines.st: Skadi looks at the chapel overlooking the town. She knows how to get to her destination.

lines.x: High Inquisitor, are we planting explosives at the hill right now?
That is correct.
On another note, I saw someone entering the chapel.
She looks very much like that... outsider we met before.
So, High Inquisitor, who is she?
An Ægir.
I heard she fought the Sea Terrors.
I'm surprised there are others who not only know about these monsters... but specialize in fighting them as well...
If we blow up the chapel... Will she be affected?
If we don't strike the evil at its root, even destroying this place won't accomplish anything.
Is that so? So we still have to find out who caused this phenomenon... And you don't know who it is yourself, High Inquisitor?
You may not ask anything that has nothing to do with the mission.
Understood...
Also, while I'm aware you don't mind, I have to say this nonetheless... The load-bearing structure of this block probably won't withstand the intensity of the explosion.
The southern half of the city may crumble any moment, and the inhabitants here still don't know anything about this.
Proceed.
Uh...
I want to evacuate the inhabitants.
I mean, to you, they may seem peculiar and problematic...
...and also things that need to be cleansed.
Inquisitor!
Uhh...!
Tell me! What is your responsibility?!
Yes!
'For Iberia's prosperity, for Iberia's defense, and for Iberia's orderliness and integrity, I shall raise my sword and my light!'
In that case, you must lay down your judgment for these men!
You want me to... lay down judgment? To sentence them to death?
High Inquisitor, when was the last time you listened to a song? Your records have all gone bad, haven't they? Your player has been gathering dust for a long time too.
I heard that song. I heard the song the Ægir was singing for these people.
That's the kind of song that Iberia should have had.
Before we kill them, I want to know the truth. Just like you were trying to find who is behind this.
Perhaps they will still mutate, but when that time comes, I will cleanse them myself, but I would like to evacuate them right now.
High Inquisitor, if I have your permission, I will evacuate everyone right away!
Is that your decision?
I don't know!
Irene, you are not just laying down decisions for those you see in front of you. Your decisions affect much more!
By sentencing the monsters to live, you also sentence the citizens to die!
I understand! I've already made decisions like this one countless of times!
Your judgment can mitigate disasters or portend ill omens! You may kill hundreds merely to save one!
I understand that, Sir!
What's at stake behind this deal, the clashes between morals and scriptures... I understand it all!
That isn't what I'm asking you.
What I'm asking you, Irene, is if you can truly bear this responsibility.
If you bring them great pain, then you too must bear this pain as well, and it will be far graver than the sins you inflict on others combined.
Do you have the courage? Will your beliefs remain steadfast when your heart is tormented? If you fail, are you certain you can stay unflinching when you witness the outcome of your decision?
I can take it... and I won't waver.
That's what I've been doing all along ever since I joined the Inquisition. I can do it. If what I do proves to be wrong, then let me receive the proper punishment. This is something I must do.
High Inquisitor! I—
Very well.
This will do. Do your duty, Inquisitor.
—Yes, High Inquisitor! I will evacuate the local inhabitants right away!
Also... The explosives left in the cabinet should be enough to level the whole of Sal Viento.
That's right.
So...
If it comes to it, I will override your judgment.
Right...
I'll get going now.

lines.st: Irene dashes off like the wind.
The High Inquisitor stands tall, unmoving, just like the reefs next to the sea.

It is only when the Inquisitors have doubts in their hearts that they have the chance to seek out the answer. However, for a young woman who has experienced little of the world's ugly side or its mirages...
She needs to be hammered and tempered. She needs to fail, to crumble.
She must first suffer. She must first abandon her unrealistic aspirations before she can truly be indomitable.
Wait.

lines.x: Who is it?
Hello, High Inquisitor.
Oh, look who's here?
Welcome, wandering singer.
Now quick, come in, come in. I knew that you would make it here.
How?
What can I say...?
Hmph. No need.
Do you carry that case everywhere you go, wandering singer?
Yes.
Then would you mind if I asked another question?
Go ahead.
What is inside this case?
A saxophone.
Ah, very well, a saxophone... a wind instrument. Quite good.
May I have the fortune to hear it? Music... this city has not heard music for a swathe of years.
Oh, no, beg pardon... I realize asudden that a wandering singer takes coin for her song.
This does stump me. You see, I fear I cannot proffer much tip to speak of.
Yet look at this old building. Humph, taking care of it is quite the challenging task, when currency stands for little here, none will collect it, and none will use it.
But I can spare energy to prepare you a luncheon. Wandering singer, what say you? Would you will to perform a little something or other?
It is in my power, at least, to invite you to eat your fill, though it may not be any gourmet delicacy...
I see you are fallen silent... Ah, might I guess: you prefer dance, rather than song?
You see, you are wearing a dancer's dress... you are talented. It has been too long since last we had a friend with such brilliant luster as you.
(...Sickening.)
If you want to listen to something, you can let me sing for now.
I came to ask a question. The person standing by you probably knows. I don't think you can escape the issue anymore.
Were my—people, here, and you, once familiar?
We naturally were.
I was not asking you.

lines.st: Gladiia turns her head away, and does not speak further.

lines.x: We aren't.
Because I have no idea now if this person is the one I originally knew.
Oh, oh...
This, too, is normal. All people may change. Seawater beats against dry land, and can turn the coast into sand, given time.
So. Your arrival, was it not her who willed it?
—Wasn't it you who wanted me to come?
Were it merely my bidding, you would not show here, correct? I am only a stranger.
I simply ask; did she will you to come, or not?
She didn't. I came on my own.
Hmm, you are not lying. I can hear when lies are told... but you are honest.
You keep asking, and I still haven't even said anything. How can you be this obnoxious?
Be calm, be calm, singer. It was not my intention. A little prudence does us all good.
Then my turn to ask.
This city's people all have great trust in you, but what happens on the seashore—
—is absolutely not normal.
Scrap-begging. Sea Terrors. The ocean.
Where are you from? You know something, don't you? What have you and her hidden from me?
Ah, hah-hah. Yes, indeed, I know a good deal more than others.
Indeed, I know of one who wants to meet you.
Meet me? Since I'm a wandering singer?
The one who cares for music is me.
Though it may invite some disbelief, the one wanting to meet you is not me.
It is a friend of mine. I bid you here on his request.
Who? He wants to meet me?
Come with me. You shall know when you see him.
After all... you have a friend here too.

lines.st: 'You wish to meet her, do you not?'
The bishop's tone makes light of it, but Skadi remains calm.

The hunter secretly grips the case's handle tight. Only fruits of progress in material science and Rhodes Island earnestly investing vast funds are to thank right now, that she does not crush the handle of this specialized 'saxophone case' apart.

lines.x: Please, in here.
As for you.
Come too. The time for your reward, too, is come.

lines.st: Skadi glances at Gladiia briefly, and does not say much.
Skadi follows over to the figure of the bishop. Unsure as to why, she's felt the whole time that the bishop has a very odd gait. Like his legs are lame, or his joints are missing.

The three descend the stairs. Shockingly, below the chapel's graves lies a cave, the staircase winding downwards by its wall, circling and circling, as if walking into some colossus's esophagus. Without a ray of light, they feel through the path on pure sense, at risk of falling into the abyss at any time.

lines.x: Regretfully, I will not light a lamp, but this should pose you no trouble, should it?

I have heard wandering singers to be quite agile by and large. Neither city nor barrens are too friendly, and those going afar easily come to suffer. One must learn the basics of protecting themselves.
Wealth cannot buy music, yet music can serve as wealth. So long as a fine song is sung, one will not starve wherever they go...
I used to yearn greatly for that sort of life, likely because I am no good at what it demands... Once I set down roots somewhere, it is very hard for me to up and leave again.

lines.st: The bishop seems to talk in soliloquy, because the two others utter not a word while descending.

lines.x: We are here.
Don't tell me we're...
Correct. On the seafloor.

lines.st: Led deep and deeper by the staircase, they finally set foot on flat ground. Even Skadi feels challenged to breathe in the sealed-off damp, until they emerge into a shaft of light, and the open scene before them reinvigorates the hunters.
The massive karst cave is filled with twisted equipment. From a hidden place above, daylight illuminates as it penetrates the ocean, permeates the cave, the faint blue making where it lies clear.

lines.x: That's—!

lines.st: How could this be.

lines.x: Specter's body floats in a vat of water, as if dead, or perhaps sound asleep.
Untold thoughts flash through Skadi's head.
Why is she here? Did Gladiia do this? What if Specter is dead? How could Gladiia allow Specter to die?! Could she truly be—
And that is your friend, isn't it?
You are called Skadi? I have not been the one following your affairs. They have handled you very well...
Especially the ones by your side. They have handled it all well, letting you uphold your mental state under such emotions.

lines.st: Skadi's eyes open wide.

lines.x: What?
They have had no time to attend to you lately, but by the humble grace of your leader here, you have come, practically as a gift to me.

lines.st: Skadi turns in anger to Gladiia, but finds no one behind her.

Gladiia is already pacing for the bishop, her step free of concern in its lightness. The speed she takes pride in, the speed rumored amongst the Abyssal Hunters... seems to be in this silent, single step.

lines.x: I am surprised you step forth first... and not that minstrel. What are you waiting for? Why did you not kill me before?
Would you be so stupid?
You unhallowed things, and your eternal condition... Your fine sense of ego, expressed without meaning. Would you be just as unconcerned if this experiment, her life, vanished here?
Confirming your results gave you confidence. Your satisfaction grinned in your words, yet you wouldn't let me see where you took her. I imagine you cannot let her die yet.
Out of us few, I'm sure her life is the one you place most importance on.
I've destroyed your experiment grounds—
To little surprise, it was your doing.
—Specter is highly valuable. Out of all the Originium infection tests you've conducted on the Abyssal Hunters, only this one specimen tells the tale.
The original sin of terrestrial lowlife beasts can be effected upon those unholy bodies of yours. Is it not so very interesting?

lines.st: The looks on Skadi and Gladiia's faces both change.

lines.x: Although... I must say, you have wrought too much damage on the site we three are in. Having committed so much refined, liquefied Originium, having applied Ægir technology... all of it, never to be seen again.
She is highly valuable indeed. My one mystery is how she fends against an Originium infection of such scope... Her spinal cord, diluted, would be enough to infect a small country.
Things have changed now. Thank you for placing her in my hands, unhallowed thing. I will lead the other two... I will discover all your final secrets.
I will prove my own success. My research. Our enemy's secrets. As well as weaknesses...
You will lead off by dying here.

lines.st: Skadi covertly applies her strength. Her puzzlement and irritation towards Gladiia pale completely to her present anger at the bishop.
Given one chance, this originator of evil will be shattered, flesh pasted.
Just one chance.

lines.x: My sparing your life testified: you are that person, one and the same. I've already executed those not worth sparing.
As it's proven how you, them, and this experiment are all implicated, you need not be alive any longer.
The Church of the Deep... you're from the Church of the Deep.
Did I permit you to address me so?
You're far too good at hiding. Each one of you is unalike. You'll say anything and everything.
Was Specter also driven to derangement... by your indoctrination and torture?
Those people outside. You exploited them. Are they experiments to you too?
Have all the people cast into the sea... been sacrifices for your tests?
Impudent.

lines.st: The bishop's figure seems to enlarge some. He may truly be angered.

lines.x: The ocean's almsgiving is no manner of exploitation. It is salvation.
I could concern myself none with them, and they would die.
The ocean gives them food, gives those unwilling to live the freedom to live on in rebirth...

lines.st: A cold shudder runs through Skadi. Sea Terrors. Sea Terrors. They become Sea Terrors.

lines.x: How could you unhallowed things hope to understand...

Enough, bishop. Have I ever shown care for your objective before? If you remember anything false, I invite you to forget it immediately. Your disgraceful conduct defies description. The only thing I mind is how much further your toxin might drift.
This is far enough.
You believed bringing one more... would let you topple me with ease?
You and your kind alike are so self-confident—so stupid.
I know that some of you chose to become Sea Terrors already. I fear that won't serve as a trump card.
Even if you can become a Seaborn—

lines.st: Skadi suddenly registers that the scent of a Seaborn has drawn near.
Moreover, it is not the bishop.

lines.x: Captain!

lines.st: Skadi leaps forward, but the Seaborn is already too close to Gladiia, much too close.

lines.x: With her speed, Gladiia is able to dodge before the sound even crosses.
But before anything, it requires that Gladiia intend to dodge.
Gladiia had not even been aware...
Haugh, hgh-eugh... (Choking on her blood)
Gla-dia. His request was this. And so I did.
Rest.

lines.st: Skadi, having witnessed the instant the monster impaled Gladiia, now watches as it extracts its limb and leaves Gladiia to crumple, ruined.
A trap. This was a trap. Skadi had long readied herself to confront a Seaborn... but why did Gladiia not notice it?
Did she allow herself to underestimate the enemy? Or did what happened to Specter destroy her calm?
Who is the hunter, and who is the hunted?
Moreover—how could—a Seaborn—

lines.x: Ishar-mla.

lines.st: (Why... why, why?)
Why did Gladiia choose to make a move here? She could've just said something to Skadi, and the two of them would've easily killed the bishop!
Can this bishop truly be not a monster? Was the scent she smelled the whole time this Seaborn, and not the bishop?
What is this thing in front of her? Is it a Sea Terror? The bishop's transformation? A Seaborn?
If it's one of her unendingly long-despised enemies, why can it talk?

lines.x: Ishar-mla. I wanted to meet you.

lines.st: Skadi does not speak. Her hand reaches for her case as she watches for the Seaborn's first move, its first possible movement.
Life and death are an instant apart. She wants the monster dead.

lines.x: Countless years buoyant. Countless years roving. Countless lived and died.
*Spit*...
Idiotic unhallowed. If the herald did not request you, all you unholy things would have been dead on the beach long ago.
Bastard...
Herald, put an end to this problem. Watching these people encroach on our ocean has always sickened me.
Are you that eager to attack them?
How many of our own kind have these things killed, these mixed-breeds brought about by Ægir?
Could it be you are—not one.
Then I will finish asking this question first.
Ishar-mla, my sister.
What...?
Sister, right now, you are not coherent of where you came from.
I come from Ægir, monster... I am your natural enemy.
You are not coherent.
Then let me tell you the truth of things.
You are we.

lines.st: What?
What?
No.
No.

lines.x: What flows through you is our blood.

lines.st: Skadi has wondered so many times. Skadi knew she had some connection to them, but she blamed every last part upon the scars the final battle left on her.
But she smells it. She knows it. She hears it. She recognizes it.

lines.x: Liar!
Herald, you... what?
Ishar-mla. Your blood is our blood.
Your bodies were, and then our blood entered.
I have innumerous blood-kin. They meet you. They die.
They smell your scent. Just as you smell our scent.
They believe their fellow kin are trapped under your skins. They bite at your hide and flesh regardless of the danger, wishing to release their own kin from your vesicles.
They have not prepared themselves, and they have not at that point the organs to realize with.
You are not trapping kin within your bodies. You are our kin.
Ishar-mla. You are we.

lines.st: It is as if the Seaborn's words carry some magical power. Skadi's cacophonous train of thought returns to the past, when she and the other hunters executed Sea Terrors together, and the thicker the stench of blood, the more excited they became, the fiercer their movements, their bodies responding.
To their same blood.

lines.x: I will wake you. You will recall that sensation. Ægir bodies are frail, and things pass too quickly. I will help you seek it out.
Stop. Stop!
But yet, you wish to know. I know you wish to know.

lines.st: Skadi wishes to know.
Skadi wishes to know just what she is in the end. She wishes to know why she must suffer so much misfortune.

lines.x: You kill us Ægir... and now you intend on being related to me?!
My blood and your blood are identical.
You smell my scent, exactly as we smell your scent.
You find us, kill us...
When we do not understand, we kill you too.
We feed the ocean. Our bodies have provided for the ocean.
Ishar-mla, we possess the same home.
You killed my family. It was you who killed my mother, my grandmother, my little sister.

lines.st: Skadi wishes to know.

lines.x: No. We wouldn't.
We do not go to Ægir cities.
So many cities, all destroyed by you!
Those cities are on our territory.
Keh! Even in the hinterland cities of Ægir, you still hunt people!
No... No.
We would not do that. Those who die in the cities are unable to provide for the many.
The Ægir attack us, we attack the Ægir. There is no difference.
Those in your territory die, by those in your territory's doing.
Lies!
Lies? I wish to know what lies means. 'Liar,' too, you said earlier. I wish to know.

lines.st: Skadi is unable to move.
Skadi seems to have touched upon the Seaborn's thought.
The Seaborn's message flows directly from its scent into the waters of her mind.
She may do this. Of course... she...

lines.x: No, no...!
Ishar-mla, the barrier between you and we is too strong. You have no way to pass on your ideas to we.
For you to know what we think, that is enough.
My body has grown, is able to speak, is able to communicate with you.
Language is bloated, not easy to use. I can only talk this way, but this way you are able to tell me.
Ishar-mla, I came to meet you.

lines.st: Skadi knows. From every part of the sum of all of it, Skadi knows—
The Seaborn would not lie.

lines.x: I and you... are not...
You often feel, the creatures around you, are unfamiliar to you.
When you travel, you are able to sense the direction of seawater, and know much of what happens in the unlit ocean.
At some times you sleep, you feel you are swimming with we many.

lines.st: Skadi's blood is already boiling.
How could this be? How could this... be?
Skadi understands.
Skadi sympathizes.
Skadi and the other hunters whisper.
Skadi and the other hunters are all monsters.

lines.x: Why did you have to tell me this? Why did you have to say this?

lines.st: She is a monster.
She murders her own kind.
She for her own kind murders her own kind.
She by her own kind murders her own kind.
The eyes of the people in the streets are filled with warmth. They don't know anything.
